There is nothing about Curio that prohibits renegotiation of an SSL connection.  In fact, it works fine.

This issue is solely about the behavior of the kernel.   The Curio kernel is only focused on task scheduling, not the implementation details of any particular protocol or application.   There is a requirement that only one task can wait to read/write on a single I/O resource.  Otherwise you get a ResourceBusy exception.   This is not unlike virtually every other async library I have ever seen, including asyncio. For example, asyncio doesn't allow more than one Future to be attached to reading or writing a given file descriptor. I don't have an issue with this restriction. 

In the context of SSL, there is a chance in certain atypical circumstances that one could get a resource conflict in the initial handshaking of a connection (for example, attempting to send/recv before the handshake is complete).   However, this conflict is easily solved using the same techniques that most resource conflicts are usually solved--use locks and events.  Curio will loudly raise an exception. It's not an error that will pass silently. 

On an already established SSL connection, there is a chance that the other end of the connection could perform a renegotiation at some point.  That works fine now.   Yes, there is an outside chance it could cause a resource conflict error, but the circumstances leading to that seem about as likely as me witnessing a dragon swoop out of the sky to pluck the Lochness monster out of Lake Michigan under the pale light of a lunar eclipse on my bike ride.  I'm not saying that would never happen--I've seen stranger things.   However, it's not something I'm really going to worry about until I witness a real-life bug report from an actual application saying that it happened.

Even then, I don't think it's the responsibility of the Curio kernel (as a scheduler) to prevent or fix it. A solution would be more likely found elsewhere--either at the application level (using some kind of more sophisticated exception handling and/or task coordination) or it would happen at the socket level (maybe having a different kind of SSL socket).

I understand the idea of the debouncing; my question is more, like, do we
expect this to be a 1% or 10% or 100% speedup? Or in principle it could
even be a slowdown if it takes longer to compute the debounce information
than it does to just unregister+reregister.
.
And agreed that having multiple readers or multiple writers is weird, but
one task reading from a file descriptor while another task writes to that
same fd is *really* normal. See e.g. that bidirectional proxy example I
used to motivate defer_cancellation. I can't imagine requiring a dup for
this case.

On Nov 12, 2016 2:29 AM, "David Beazley" <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> The rationale for the debouncing is that if a task reads once, there's a
> pretty good chance it's going to read again. For example, reading some
> headers on a request and then reading a request body. If a task writes,
> there's a good chance it's going to write again. For example, writing a
> large response in chunks. I consider both of these cases to be real-world
> scenarios--not just something from an echo server.
>
> I consider any situation where two tasks are blocked on the same
> descriptor to be an error. It's already an error in the selectors module so
> far as I can tell. Real screwy things happen if you do things like this
> with threads as well (at least in my experience). If someone really wants
> multiple tasks to be doing something with the same descriptor, they should
> probably use os.dup() on it and work with that.
>
> The case of closing/reopening should be looked at though. That is a bug.
>
> As a side-note: some other async frameworks seem to make a similar
> "optimization" by simply leaving the file descriptor attached to the event
> loop and performing their own internal buffering. This ties it to the
> buffer-bloat problem. There is peril everywhere.
>
> I will ponder this for a bit...

> what about adding a switch somewhere that users could toggle? Something like a Quantity.AUTO_REDUCE_DIMENSIONS class member?

Supporting automatic dimensional reduction via an option sound like a nice idea to me (especially for interactive use). 

IIRC pint has no options on Quantity class level. Instead options are set on unit-registry level, e.g. `autoconvert_offset_to_baseunit` (in [registry.py](https://github.com/hgrecco/pint/blob/master/pint/registry.py)). I think your idea could also be added on registry level.

Note that I am not a pint maintainer but only an occasional contributor.

Numba will take a function designed for scalars, and after an annotation,
produce a function that will take vectors. It will JIT compile the function
in the background to C using LLVM, so it will run at the same speed as C
code.

To use this with dfply, you will have to have two functions: the outer one
is annotated with @make_symbolic, this calls the inner one which has been
vectorised with Numba.

> inputs as well as scalars. If you run into problems finding the right API
> calls to operate on vectors, just loop over the inputs.
>
> Remember to annotate your function with @make_symbolic, see section in
> dfply readme "Extending dfply with custom functions".
>

Hi,

Yes, I agree,  it's not an ideal solution, and there might be other and better workarounds as well.

The hard fact on the windows platform is that the threads are taken away silently.
Destructors are called (but threads are not alive then).

A solution more in your direction here would be to simply stop the worker-thread when the user remove the timers,
 then 
re-start the thread when the first timer is added (similar to the initial situation, first timer added).

I also tested out this approach, It do work, - but I left it out since it was somewhat more involving changing code that is working (ref. timer_global_clock::remove(..)
 .. at the end of the function, check if the tm.size() ==0, and then shutdown=true, wait().

If that is a solution that could be an acceptable as a PR, I can make the mod's needed, incl. tests to this one.

**Addressing the larger problem, the silent win-thread-kill in ExitProcess:**

The take-away here seems to be that we can't rely on any threads alive in any of the static destructors. As stated in doc, there is no signal given at this point, that we could hook on to.

thread-local storage(memory) is not the main issue here I think, since it's reclaimed anyway. The problem occurs if those objects maintains external resources not reclaimed by win-os.
On windows, clearly, one should stay away from tls for all objects except internal state/memory etc.

 Adding platform specific code to do the destructor-work for dlib statics can be done,
 like
 'if in ExitProcess..., then skip lock/waits etc. and just cleanup open *external* resources, since the memory etc. is recovered by the win-os anyway.)'

Another thing to checkout is the standard **atexit** function, but there is a hard requirement that the .dll's that register a callback to it-self, should still be loaded at the time when the at-exit is called.
(We basically can't promise that, since it's out of our control)

Without this issue resolved, we can't use the standard version dlib in any python extension module we create on the windows-platform;
 - it will hang any process that uses it, 
so I will do my best to figure out a solution that could be acceptable. 

If anybody else have other ideas, or even proven concepts that fixes this, -please let us know.

Br Sigbjørn

We can't help you with the issue you're having if you leave out crucial information. What happens after 5 minutes? Did the problem occur only once or can it be reproduced? It looks like you left out part of the code, where do you use `pytz`?

---

From what I can tell from this snippet, there's a couple of pretty big issues with your code. I suggest you tackle these *first*, because they'll make debugging a lot easier.

* You're importing `time` from `datetime`, then importing the `time` module, which overwrites `datetime.time`. I suggest importing `datetime` 'as' `dt`:

  ```python
  import datetime as dt
  import time
  ```
  
  You will then have to prefix some objects with `dt`:

  ```python
  ...
  T3 = (dt.datetime.utcnow().strftime("%H:%M:%S"))
  ...
  ```

* You use way too much whitespace. If you use whitespace and blank lines sparsely, they can greatly improve readability:

  ```python
  import datetime as dt
  import time

  from guizero import App, Text
  import pytz


  def update_writing():
      T1 = (time.strftime("%H:%M:%S"))
      #T3 = (time.strftime("%H:%M:%S"))
      T3 = (datetime.utcnow().strftime("%H:%M:%S"))

      writing.set("LOCAL TIME")
      writing1.set(T1)
      writing2.set("UTC")
      writing3.set(T3)
           
      app.after(200, update_writing)
  ```

  * Standard library imports are grouped together; then a single blank line; then third-party library imports

  * Keep two blank lines in between top-level function definitions

  * Retrieving and formatting the current time is grouped together; then a single blank line; followed by a block of code that updates the text widgets

  Just try to remember to group related actions together and separate code blocks by blank lines. 
  This will also take the strain off of others reading your code, who can now focus on the actual code, not the formatting.

* `writing` isn't a noun. It's pretty confusing to use a verb as a variable name, so I recommend:

  * `writing` → `text_widget`
  * `writing1` → `text_widget1`
  * `writing2` → `text_widget2`
  * `writing3` → `text_widget3`

  In a similar manner, `update_writing` → `update_text_widgets`

* You're declaring variables in a global scope. That's a Really Bad Idea™. You would be much better off defining them in the scope of a class. As you can see, it breaks, because Python doesn't know those widgets exist.

* You've put unnecessary braces around the `T1` and `T3` assignments:

  * `T1 = (time.strftime("%H:%M:%S"))` → `T1 = time.strftime("%H:%M:%S")`
  * `T3 = (datetime.utcnow().strftime("%H:%M:%S"))` → `T3 = datetime.utcnow().strftime("%H:%M:%S")`

Guizero v0.4 changes
----------------------

* You're using the deprecated `Text.set` method. You should use the `value` property instead:

  ```python
  writing.value = "LOCAL TIME"
  writing1.value = T1
  ...
  ```

* There's the [`App.repeat`](https://github.com/lawsie/guizero/blob/version-0.4/guizero/Widget.py#L18) method, which automatically calls an object every *n* seconds.

* The `bgcolor` argument has been renamed to `bg`.

Rewrite

Here's a Guizero v0.4 idiomatic version:

```python
import datetime as dt
import time

from guizero import App, Text
import pytz


class Application:
    def __init__(self, app):
        self._app = app
        self._create_widgets()
        self._app.repeat(200, self._update_text_widgets)

    def _create_widgets(self):
        self._text_widget = Text(self._app, text="", color="red", size=50)
        self._text_widget1 = Text(self._app, text="", color="red", size=50)
        self._text_widget2 = Text(self._app, text="T1", color="red", size=50)
        self._text_widget3 = Text(self._app, text="", color="red", size=50)

    def _update_text_widgets(self):
        t1 = time.strftime("%H:%M:%S")
        t2 = dt.datetime.utcnow().strftime("%H:%M:%S")

        self._text_widget.value = "LOCAL TIME"
        self._text_widget1.value = t1
        self._text_widget2.value = "UTC"
        self._text_widget3.value = t2


def main():
    root = App(height=1000, width=1000, bg="black")
    app = Application(root)
    root.display()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

And finally, here's a version without classes, in case you're not comfortable using classes yet:

```
import datetime as dt
import time

from guizero import App, Text
import pytz


def _update_text_widgets():
    t1 = time.strftime("%H:%M:%S")
    t2 = dt.datetime.utcnow().strftime("%H:%M:%S")

    text_widget.set("LOCAL TIME")
    text_widget1.set(t1)
    text_widget2.set("UTC")
    text_widget3.set(t2)

    app.after(200, _update_text_widgets)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    app = App(height=1000, width=1000, bg="black")

    text_widget = Text(app, text="", color="red", size=50)
    text_widget1 = Text(app, text="", color="red", size=50)
    text_widget2 = Text(app, text="T1", color="red", size=50)
    text_widget3 = Text(app, text="", color="red", size=50)

    app.after(200, _update_text_widgets)
    app.display()

```

Note: you don't have to include the `if __name__ == "__main__":` statement, it's just good practice.

Hi @nikhilkalige!

I have not actually used the monkeypatch.  It should definitely enable you to get Exhale to generate multiple projects as per the README instructions, I think the `breathe_projects_source` is crucial in how @mithro is able to get around me not having `:project:` calls.

However, if it is causing you problems, since you need to track that file locally here is what I suggest you do to further patch things to suit your needs.

1. Track the file `exhale_multiproject_monkeypatch.py` next to your `conf.py` and make sure to append to `sys.path` so you can `import` it.  Making sure that you do this before declaring anything else.
2. At the bottom of his patch, **after** Exhale has generated everything but still inside the `for project in breathe_projects` loop, manually splice in your own `:project:` commands.  It won't be pretty, but should at least enable you to move forward in the interim.  It may look something like this

```py
for project in breathe_projects:
    # leave everything at the beginning of the loop as is
    # this stuff is what actually gets past Exhale's obsessive
    # requirements enabling you to generate multiple APIs
    #  ....
    #
    # Generate the full API!
    try:
        exhale.deploy.explode()
    except:
        exhale.utils.fancyError("Exhale: could not generate reStructuredText documents :/")

    # This is where you come in
    containmentFolder = app.config.exhale_args["containmentFolder"]
    # traverse the *ENTIRE* containmentFolder subtree for '.rst' files
    # you need to open _EVERY_ file (ugh) and replace directives now
```

So use `os.walk` or whatever.  But basically, you should be able to regular expression replace the contents of the files.  Read the file in, now replace any line of the form `^(.. doxygen.*::.*)$`.  Now since you have the `project` variable, you can replace it.  This is all very wishy-washy, but the idea is you want to turn something like

```rst
.. doxygenfunction:: namespace::foo
```

into

```rst
.. doxygenfunction:: namespace::foo
   :project: {insert project here}
```

Just be absolutely sure that there are exactly **three** spaces before your inserted `:project:` there since Exhale uses three spaces for other potential specifiers added and reStructuredText demands consistent whitespace.

I hope that is helpful in providing a bypass.  Unfortunately this PR is not in a state to use in production, and won't be for a while :cry:

Please feel free to ask for clarifications if that is not clear enough.  Replacing ``.. doxygenANYTHING`` by capturing it and inserting `r"\1\n   :project: {project}\n".format(project=project)` is the name of the game.  Doing this for every single file is unfortunate, but in reality the generation of the files by Exhale (and subsequent modification by you) is actually quite fast (**tip** file I/O is **much** faster in python **3**).  What takes a long time is Sphinx parsing these files / translating them into HTML.

Note, the format of the SSH-config specifier changed in Netmiko 1.4.0. See the 1.4.0 release notes for new host specifier:

https://github.com/ktbyers/netmiko/releases/tag/v1.4.0

The implications of this change are:

1. Default send_config_set() will reduce the delay between config command writes by a factor of 10 (to 50 milliseconds per command). Which I should have done earlier independent of this as it was way too slow...

2. If you pass the fast_cli=True argument to ConnectHandler then.

    a. global_delay_factor changes to the lesser of global_delay_factor and delay_factor. This implies global_delay_factor less than 1 should cause things to be faster.

    b. The default global_delay_factor goes to .1 which implies sleep times will be reduced by a factor of 10 (by default).

    c. Default timeout for send_command will also be reduced by a factor of 10 (from about 100 seconds to about 10 seconds).

    d. send_config_set() will have no delay between config commands on a write action i.e. it will write the config commands to the device as fast at it can.

Hi,

Technically it's all the same and we're just discussing API design. As long as you're consistent it doesn't matter. I'll offer my 2 cents. You have 3 obvious choices: 

1) Embed jitter in backoff as I did:
```python
def backoff(start, stop, count=None, factor=2, jitter=lambda x:x):
```
2) Provide a function as you recommend:
```python
def add_jitter(iter):
    return map(full_jitter, iter)

x = add_jitter(backoff(1,10)) 
```
3) Let people just do it themselves:
```python
[full_jitter(i) for i in backoff(1,10)]
```

IMHO, jitter is so important to backoff that it should be the way people use it. Therefore, (1) makes it glaringly obvious to users. I should have made `full_jitter` the default and force users to turn it off with `None`. If someone wants to jitter a different int stream they can easily do (3). So my hunch is that (2) would be of limited value. 

I'm cool with whatever you decide. Also, you might want to add 1 or 2 more jitter functions. `full_jitter` looks lonely as the only option.

Just for reference (mostly for myself), the tradeoffs I've outlined before as a table:

|                      | Block based interface                                 | Stream based interface                                     |
|----------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| Block based storage  | *Best case*                                           | Has to write the whole dataset instead of the changes only |
| Stream based storage | Keep all data in memory OR re-read before every write | *Best case*                                                |

- Stream based storage: JSONStorage
- Block based storage: MemoryStorage
- Stream based interface: current approach
- Block based interface: proposal

- `sudo pip install psutil`   (install at system level)
- login as root (`sudo su -` or `su -`) then `pip install psutil`  (again, install at system level)
- `pip install psutil --user`  (install at user level)

Regarding our discussion,
> My concern is if we can't get per-CPU temp then I'm not sure what a single CPU temp value should represent on a multi CPU platform. The average of all CPUs temperatures?

 I am unsure how to approach the multi-CPU configuration. To my best knowledge, there are no official Apple systems with 2 CPUs that have ever been produced (maybe some Hackintosh). telepot does allow you to supply `chat_id` and `message_id`. from the [Reference](https://github.com/nickoala/telepot/blob/master/REFERENCE.md#telepot-Bot) :

editMessageText(msgid_form, text, parse_mode=None, disable_web_page_preview=None, reply_markup=None)
```
msgid_form can be:

- a tuple of (chat_id, message_id)
- a tuple of (inline_message_id)
- a single value - inline_message_id

so, basically just call it this way:
```python
bot.editMessageText((chat_id, message_id), new_text)
```

IPDB works on ip addresses in the `commit()` after the interface `flags` are set. The flags in the transaction are `4098` — the interface should be **down** after `commit()`

What could happen:
1. The interface is set **up** (thus implicitly bringing up a link-local IPv6 address) again after it is set down by IPDB and addresses are flushed, but before IPDB checks the `ipaddr` target (rare, but still possible case)
2. There are some addresses left while in the transaction, despite the explicit flush (can not imagine, how it may happen) — then it is a library bug.

However, I do agree that we should have a timeout on `_poll_until_not()`. What about this?

```python
    def _poll_until_not(url, pending_statuses, err_msg):
        result, t0 = None, time.time()
        while result is None or result['status'] in pending_statuses:
            assert (time.time() - t0 < 5 * 60), "Polling timeout"
            time.sleep(0 if result is None else 2)
            result, _, _ = _do_request(url, err_msg=err_msg)
        return result
```

The core issue that started 4.0.3 is that I discovered that some DBs like PostgreSQL and MySQL will send column names (SQLDescribeCol) in UTF-16LE *regardless* of the database / client encoding.  Undocumented as far as I can tell.  Since the encoding is configurable, I had to add a 3rd encoding configuration which now defaults to UTF-16LE.

During this emergency change I accidentally passed a buffer length instead of a character length.

The first bad commit is 3a2fe35e6. Also I had to temporary comment `typedef unsigned long ULONG` in `/usr/include/sqltypes.h` otherwise I have a conflict type with `ULONG` defined in the win32 library. 

With that little change on `/usr/include/sqltypes.h` I can build a703e39 on both Cygwin x32 and Cygwin x64.

I stated earlier:
>> In effect IDF0 -> IDF1 -> IDF2 -> follows the chronological development of the class.

This leads to the conclusion that the code would have greater clarity if it was a single class `IDF` that has all the functionality.

Another approach would be to start with and base class and to create a series of child classes, each of which has greater functionality that is obvious from the class name. The classic example that is usually waved around is shown below. It comes from computer graphics

    #  much pseudocode here
    class basicpoint(object) 
        x
        y
    
    class coloredpoint(point)  
        # rgb
        red
        green
        blue

    class drawpoint(coloredpoint)
        def draw_thepoint()
            # some code that draws the point on the screen in color
        
I think  IDF0 -> IDF1 -> IDF2 has such a logic to it. Especially if I give them more meaningful names and shuffle the code around a bit. In fact that was my original thinking behind it. I did not give them meaningful names at that time, because I did not know what I was doing until I did it :-)

The proof is in the pudding. So I need to look at the code to see if such a direction is viable and will actually add clarity. If the code does not look amenable to such a modification, we can just make a single class and be done with it.

If you don't need to account for signed numbers, you can use `UNSIGNED` and you shouldn't need to use `TYPESAFE`; you won't get any performance penalty - in fact, if you move to `UNSIGNED | INT` the number searching is the fastest.

Here is an improved ExceptHook as Python code - can be copied, used and modified freely in user's app:

```python
from cefpython3 import cefpython as cef
import traceback
import codecs
import time
import os
import wx

# Globals
g_ExceptHook_called = False
g_ExceptHook_original_msg = ""


def ExceptHook(exc_type, exc_value, exc_trace):
    """Global except hook to exit app cleanly on error.
    This hook does the following: in case of exception write it to
    the "error.log" file, display it to the console, shutdown CEF
    and exit application immediately by ignoring "finally" (_exit()).
    """

    # Sometimes calling CEF shutdown procedures may cause another
    # exception and in such case let's kill the process immediately
    # and display the original exception message.
    global g_ExceptHook_called
    global g_ExceptHook_original_msg
    if g_ExceptHook_called:
        print("[excepthook.py] ExceptHook called recursively, ignoring"
              " and shutting down Python process immediately")
        print("[excepthook.py] Original exception below:")
        print("\n" + g_ExceptHook_original_msg)
        # noinspection PyProtectedMember
        os._exit(1)
        return
    g_ExceptHook_called = True

    # Print exception
    print("[excepthook.py] Catched exception:")
    msg = "".join(traceback.format_exception(exc_type, exc_value,
                                             exc_trace))

    # Log the error to a file
    error_file = cef.GetAppPath("error.log")
    encoding = cef.GetAppSetting("string_encoding") or "utf-8"
    if type(msg) == bytes:
        msg = msg.decode(encoding=encoding, errors="replace")
    try:
        with codecs.open(error_file, mode="a", encoding=encoding) as fp:
            fp.write("\n[%s] %s\n" % (
                    time.strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"), msg))
    except:
        print("[excepthook.py] WARNING: failed writing to error file: %s" % (
                error_file))

    # Convert error message to ascii before printing, otherwise
    # you may get error like this:
    # | UnicodeEncodeError: 'charmap' codec can't encode characters
    msg = msg.encode("ascii", errors="replace")
    msg = msg.decode("ascii", errors="replace")
    g_ExceptHook_original_msg = msg
    print("\n"+msg)

    # There is a strange bug on Mac. Sometimes except message is not
    # printed if QuitMessageLoop and Shutdown were called before the print
    # message above. See Issue #382 for details.
    print("[excepthook.py] Shutdown CEF processes cleanly")
    cef.QuitMessageLoop()
    cef.Shutdown()

    # Display the exception message once again as the CEF procedures
    # above might have generated lots of logs and the exception message
    # is lost among those. Note that this block of code may not be
    # called in some cases. Sometimes CefShutdown will just exit
    # process immediately. This seems to happen when throwing an error
    # in KeyboardHandler.OnKeyEvent on Mac.
    print("[excepthook.py] Original exception below:")
    print("\n" + msg)

    # Exit process immediately.
    # noinspection PyProtectedMember
    os._exit(1)
```

> Make QR usage the default please. I know you want to decrease HA dependencies and I want to help with that, but I think HAP python should be comfortably usable as a stand-alone package. So users should get QR by default and disabling it should be optional.

My initial approach might have been a bit drastic. I took that from the way `pyqrcode` handles it for [png output](https://pythonhosted.org/PyQRCode/moddoc.html#pyqrcode.QRCode.png). I though about how this could be improved and came up with two options:
 1. Install requirements during first run if `qr_code=True`
 2. Define an additional `extras_require`. That way the QR code install would just be `pip3 install HAP-python[QRCode]`

@pombreda that's actually what this code tries to do ;-)  The problem here is that the line endings in the replacement text has no relation to the line endings in the file. We shouldn't try to  change line endings in the file, and we should definitely not introduce mixed line endings.  If the file has consistent line endings, then it is sufficient to look at the first line.  If the file does not have consistent line endings, then no choice of line endings will be correct (making it sufficient to also look at the first line only).  IIRC then the current code always adds \r\n on windows, which will corrupt files containing only \n (I seem to remember that I found this in a makefile, but it's a while ago).

I confirm this issue. The upload test is way too small for any comprehensive results when the machine running the test has a really fast connection.

The upload test size should be at least configurable if not automatically changeable based on the download speed (I believe the flash client on the speedtest.net website calculates automatically how big the upload test should be based on the download speed).

Right now this tool, while extremely useful for slow connections, it's pretty much useless for fast connections.

@NotANormalNerd the issue described here is not about inconsistency due to Python versions or CPU/RAM. This is about [TCP slow start](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Slow-start) and that's why speedtest.net will do larger tests on machines with faster connections.

When you have 1gbit or 10gbit uplinks, running an upload test of only a few mbytes it's impossible to measure how much bandwidth your uplink can handle since the test is completed in about a second or two, and [TCP slow start](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Slow-start) will take up to a few seconds to fully fill the available bandwidth of the uplink.

I believe the easiest way to resolve this is to simply allow the user to define the test sizes to compensate for that.

I too confirm this. I'd be happy to contribute to try to fix this though...

Found this speedtest.net under-the-hood guide:
https://support.speedtest.net/hc/en-us/articles/203845400-How-does-the-test-itself-work-How-is-the-result-calculated-

I've setup a single board computer (odroid) which is running Ubuntu 14.04.3 LTS. It's connected to a 200/100.

The upload test was way out to begin with, but by decreasing the number of threads to 4, increase the time of the test and increase the size of the files it's now only 15% off. Increasing the file size and test time might minimse the TCP slow start effect.

A few ideas to increase it further would be to:
1. Develop a method to decide the speed of the connection
2. Both the download and upload tests are configured based on the initial test.
3. Instead of timing the test from the start of the first thread being spawned to when the last thread is finished each thread is timed individually. The two fastest and the bottom 1/4 results are removed and the remaining results are averaged out (according to the algorithm described by the link above)
4. Don't spawn any more threads after the configured test period is over, but allow the running tests to finalise before ending the test.
5. I think the test result is affected negatively since the overhead of generating files to send is included in the overall test time. Timing each thread separately might solve this. Otherwise I was thinking that one could try to pregenerate the files to be sent instead of generating them on the fly to get rid of the overhead. I'm afraid that caching will kick in and distort the result though.
6. Input to the additions would be to have a closer look at how the speedtest.net test is executed using the browser. Wireshark to do some traffic analysis might be helpful. I.e. the link above mention that the browser based test is using four threads for connections above 4 Mbits otherwise two threads are used. How big is the chunks and buffer sizes depending on connection speed? Would be good input...

> I don't understand how this could fix the pip issue on Windows, which didn't even notice was mentioned when mail first came in.

The issue is:
- pip install starts to install
- The native code is tried to be compiled, with the installed compiler, typical Visual Studio
- Visual Studio can be localized, and output warnings in the current locale, including non-ascii characters
- The implicit cast is shown as a warning by Visual Studio
- Pip is unable to encode the output of the build due to that warning, and fails (bug in pip)

The final result is that pip9 will fail to install any python package in Windows with Visual Studios localized for any python extension that produce certain compilation warnings.

I just want to provide some clarification:
- SecretStorage really needs dbus-python, it won't function without it.
- You can install dbus-python from Ubuntu repos (packages `python-dbus` and `python3-dbus`), then you won't need to install it from PyPI (actually, you can install SecretStorage itself from Ubuntu repos too).
- When installing dbus-python from PyPI, it needs to be compiled from source.
- For compilation, it needs a `dbus-1` pkg-config package (i.e. a `dbus-1.pc` file). On Ubuntu, it is provided in `libdbus-1-dev` package. One will need `dbus-glib-1.pc` from `libdbus-glib-1-dev` package too.

In this case the 64 bytes limit applies to your input/ouput/feature reports. 
  
 Feel free to post the 33 bytes HID report descriptor, on Windows only 
 drivers have access to this descriptor. 
  
 Usually, unless you'd be really familiar with HID report descriptors ( 
 http://www.usb.org/developers/hidpage/HID1_11.pdf) you'd use macros 
 provided by your firmware SDK. Reason is HID reports schema is binary with 
 bit field level fields. 
  
 The rough way to design a HID report is to use the USB SIG HID report tool, 
 it's a GUI, but honestly is far from easy to use, this would only help you 
 for simple reports. 
  
 I remember I saw some script based tools on the web, you'd describe our 
 reports in XML/Json form and you'd get a header from it, but if you are 
 into firmware, I'd rather recommend getting C macros once you'd feel 
 familiar with the reports. 
  
 Other important item is validating your binary descriptor, also I remember 
 seeing some Linux parsers before, a quick search on google gave me this: 
 http://eleccelerator.com/usbdescreqparser/

I think it makes sense to create your own script for your use-case (which was interesting to hear, btw!), but unfortunaly(?) there is no currently way to say whether the handshake is done as a part of communication. The basic workflow would however look like this, which would assuming there are no other limitations on the device would go like this:
1. Initial discovery handshake (which is done currently every time something is sent out)
2. Keep polling the device until the time limit hits (the device will stop answering to requests if there has been no handshake for a while, but can't recall the details, e.g. https://github.com/aholstenson/miio/ does handshake only every X minutes)
3. Do a handshake only when necessary.

Adding a cache instead of sending a handshake for every request is something we could merge assuming it won't break anything. Making it send the handshake for each requests was just an easy way out at that point.

You could do this to fix this issue:
```python
from pyswagger import App
from pyswagger.primitives import MimeCodec
from pyswagger.primitives.codec import PlainCodec

mime_codec = MimeCodec()
mime_codec.register('text/html', PlainCodec())
app = App.load("your_swagger.json", mime_codec=mime_codec)
app.prepare(strict=True)
```

It's because for an API service, it's not common to return 'text/html' as content-type. It should usually be 'application/json'. What's your use case to use 'text/html' as content-type for api service?

Sorry, my last comment was wrong.  It could be implemented as you suggest.

However, I don't like this behavior to be the default.  I'd prefer to have the dependencies explicitly be marked.  Furthermore, this behavior would mean that internal data structures of pytest get modified even for packages that don't even use pytest-dependency, if only pytest-dependency is installed.

I will rather add a configuration switch `automark_dependency` to be set in the ini file.  If set to `False`, the default, the current behavior will be retained, e.g. dependencies will need to be marked explicitly.  If set to `True`, it will have the same effect as if all test are implicitly decorated with `@pytest.mark.dependency()`.

The only downside of this method is that it would include it with the current directory name (tests). I'm not sure if that's a good idea...

Renaming the directory might be an option though.

Well I made some good progress on this. I need to clear up the minimal configuration for nginx, and understand exactly why each piece is needed. But one thing that does resolve this issue, is using a keep-alive message (no hacking of the nginx config). 

Without a keep alive message the nginx server will use `keepalive_timeout` when its value is reached, resulting in 504 gateway timeout. With a keep-alive message you're periodically publishing something to the stream. This prevents the connection from closing. The way I've implemented this, currently, is by using Celery-beat. 

```
@celery.task                                                                                                  
def publish_keep_alive():                                                                                     
    sse.publish({'message': 'keep alive'})
``` 
```
celery.conf.CELERYBEAT_SCHEDULE = {                                                                       
    'keep-alive-every-30-seconds': {                                                                      
        'task': 'chat_app.periodic_tasks.publish_keep_alive',              
        'schedule': 30.0,                                                                                 
        'args': None                                                                                      
    },                                                                                                    
}
```
This needs to be done for each channel. But I would enjoy finding a solution not using Celery. If anyone has ideas... I think it'd be better for the docs to have a lower-level solution than having to say  "hey, you're going to have to use Celery for this" :)

We don't parse any XML. Device support has to be manually introduced, like you can see with the switches in actors.py. But you can mix different types quite easily and create new classes. So for example the class for the power-meter-switch inherits it's capabilities from the generic HMDevice class, but also some from one of the Switch classes, and somehow also of sensors.
So basically we're trying to rebuild what the XML-files are telling us with Python-Objects inheriting from one another.

The problem I see in your case is, that EQ3 probably will never add the XML files for the devices you build to the CCU. So they will only work with Homegear.

Personally I think it would make more sense to let them use the MQTT protocol. I'm pretty sure there are already quite a lot Arduino-projects doing just that. And with MQTT those devices work with HASS out of the box, and also pretty much any other application capable of speaking MQTT. As far as I know there's even somehow the possibility to talk MQTT with the CCU (Google hm2mqtt). Even Homegear supports MQTT.
So instead of limiting yourself to follow the specification of a vendor that's mostly active in the german speaking area, why not go global with a protocol that's spoken internationally?

Good to know. So essentially it would be best wo work through your list and add all known variants to the appropriate devicetype. The ones that are there already, are ones that are known to work. Adding the missing ones would then enable support for those where multiple variants are available.

@bmbouter Thanks for your opinion. I appreciate every point of view. Of course everything you say is correct and all the points are valid, but let me put it another way. I assume that you came here because you got this cacert.pem is missing error after upgrading to ``python-redmine`` v1.4.0. You got it because you didn't upgrade your version of ``python-requests`` for some time, I believe you should have had a version below 2.4.0 because that was the version where they moved certificates into separate package. 

So, what if ``python-redmine`` didn't bundle the ``python-requests`` ? Everything would continue to work of course, but when would you upgrade your ``python-requests``, how long would you continue to use this outdated but working version installed in your system ? How many security issues have been already discovered and which of them affects your outdated ``python-requests`` version ?

What I'm trying to say is that you, as a user of ``python-redmine``, are only interested in this concrete software and you only check if there are new versions of ``python-redmine``, you don't check for new versions of ``python-requests`` or ``certifi`` or whatever dependency a software might have (well maybe you do, but believe me, most people don't) and this is a really big problem, it creates a lot more security issues than you described.

A lot of people only upgrade ``python-redmine`` and don't care about whether there are new versions of ``python-requests``, but with this new model, when ``python-requests`` is bundled within ``python-redmine`` users don't need to check for new versions of the dependencies anymore, I will do this for them, it is my responsibility, as the author of the library, to provide a secure software. You can say that I don't need to bundle ``python-requests`` for that, I just need to change the required version number for the most recent secure version, but that would still require to release a new version of ``python-redmine`` plus if there is any other software that uses ``python-requests`` it might just stop working and a user would have more problems (creating separate virtualenvs etc.).

Finally, I believe that bundling ``python-requests`` is a right decision, I also believe that ``certifi`` should also be bundled BUT I have a proposal to make power users happy. In the new version (to be released) ``python-redmine`` will check if there is a ``python-requests`` or ``certifi`` installed in the system and their version(s) are greater than the ones used by ``python-redmine`` internally, ``python-redmine`` will use them, if not it will fall back to the bundled ones. This way users can instantly upgrade ``python-requests`` and ``certifi`` if needed.

Waiting for your opinions about this proposal.

Regarding being able to restart the evaluation, or at least the population - that's pretty much exactly what I was testing for with checkpointing; it does work with non-distributed NEAT currently, and I suspect that anyone wanting to do (serious) distributed computing is going to have a big enough task at hand that the loss due to not checkpointing will be much more unacceptable.

If the primary detects that it needs to reinitialize the connections to the secondaries, then it should probably put any genomes that it sent out and didn't get back fitnesses for in a pool that, once other genomes are done, is sent out again if it hasn't received fitnesses for them by then. Alternatively or as well, if the secondaries keep track of what fitness information they've gathered so far and don't automatically completely shut down or otherwise become unavailable, some mechanism to inquire for this (or even simply having them send back a pickled dict of genome id vs fitness as the first thing in the queue going back to the primary node, once a connection was re-established; `isinstance` or similar should be usable to tell this apart from the usual secondary responses).

In regard to why not always stop the secondary process, several thoughts:
- Startup time for the evaluation function. Depending on the evaluation function, setting it up may require significant time (loading in large files, doing preliminary calculations, or whatever). It may well be helpful to keep around the secondaries to try another run with adjusted, say, mutation rates. Another example - pypy should work rather faster if it's able to keep going for a while; for short runs, it's frequently slower than cpython.
- Startup difficulty: In the current setup, as is probably needed to keep it independent of the local configuration, the distributed.py code has no way to restart the secondaries, for instance - it's dependent on the calling code for that, and it would be rather more convenient for the calling code if it didn't have to restart the run again and again, potentially from the start, because the secondaries exited.
- If the connection between the secondary and the primary is lost, the original code would have the secondary in a loop; the current code at least sometimes detects this and exits the loop, but the question is how to get the secondary reconnected - something that isn't possible exiting the loop equals exiting the process.
- If the secondary is still around, and has kept a cache of genome id vs fitness results, then if the primary sends it the genome in question again, it can just respond with the fitness.

The stopevent idea should work - good thought! The queues are what's currently working ally, the best, as far as I can tell... urr... but that's with queues that are continuously going. It may be hard for it to tell the difference between an empty queue and a broken queue. Hmm... currently, one difficulty is that the primary doesn't actually know how many secondaries it is supposed to have available. If it had that information, it could just stick a series of "stop" tokens on the queue going to the secondaries and wait to receive an equal number of acknowledging "I'm stopping" tokens on the return queue.

Your understanding of the csrf protection seems to be correct. The csrf protection is achieved by the csrf token in your jwt (stored as httponly) must match the X-CSRF-HEADER you pass in as well. The reason your example is working is by default this extension only applies csrf protection to http methods that are by convention supposed to change state (post, put, patch, delete). You can change this with the ```JWT_CSRF_METHODS``` config option to include get requests as well, although if you aren't doing any state modifications in a get request it strictly speaking shouldn't be required (but obviously wouldn't hit anything to have there).

I think I see the scenario where you're trying to patch several features of a model class, but the example you gave doesn't look like it would actually work. Here's my attempt to write a small example that actually requires several patches. Let's imagine that we want to write a `Car.discount_slow()` method that applies a discount to all slow cars, and validates those discounted prices before saving them.

    class Car(models.Model):
        make = models.ForeignKey(Manufacturer)
        model = models.CharField(max_length=25, blank=True, null=True)
        speed = models.IntegerField()
        price = models.IntegerField()
    
        def format_speed(self):
            return '{0} km/h'.format(self.speed)
    
        def validate_price(self):
            # Call some expensive API, raise ValidationError
            pass
    
        def clean(self):
            super(Car, self).clean()
            self.validate_price()
    
        @classmethod
        def discount_slow(cls):
            for car in Car.objects.filter(speed__lt=100):
                car.price /= 2
                car.full_clean()
                car.save()

Here's my modified version of the test from your example:

    @patch.object(Car, 'clean_fields')
    @patch.object(Car, 'validate_price')
    @patch.object(Car, 'objects', new_callable=MockSet)
    @patch.object(Car, 'save')
    def test_car_validates_price_in_discount_slow(self,
                                                  save_mock,
                                                  objects_mock,
                                                  validate_price_mock,
                                                  clean_fields_mock):
        fast_price = 80000
        fast = Car(price=fast_price, speed=300)
        slow_price = 5000
        slow = Car(price=slow_price, speed=55)
        objects_mock.add(fast, slow)

        Car.discount_slow()

        self.assertEqual(fast_price, fast.price)  # no discount
        self.assertEqual(slow_price / 2, slow.price)
        save_mock.assert_called_once_with()
        clean_fields_mock.assert_called_once_with(exclude=[])
        validate_price_mock.assert_called_once_with()

We don't want the developer to need so many patches, right? The changes in pull request #28 will patch `objects` and `save`, along with any related models. They should also be compatible with other patches, you just have to apply `mocked_relations` on the outside, because it swallows the mocks it creates.

    @mocked_relations(Car)
    @patch.object(Car, 'clean_fields')
    @patch.object(Car, 'validate_price')
    def test_car_validates_price_in_discount_slow2(self,
                                                   validate_price_mock,
                                                   clean_fields_mock):
        fast_price = 80000
        fast = Car(price=fast_price, speed=300)
        slow_price = 5000
        slow = Car(price=slow_price, speed=55)
        Car.objects.add(fast, slow)

        Car.discount_slow()

        self.assertEqual(fast_price, fast.price)  # no discount
        self.assertEqual(slow_price / 2, slow.price)
        Car.save.assert_called_once_with()
        clean_fields_mock.assert_called_once_with(exclude=[])
        validate_price_mock.assert_called_once_with()

Another option is [`@patch.multiple()`][multi].

    @mocked_relations(Car)
    @patch.multiple(Car, clean_fields=DEFAULT, validate_price=DEFAULT)
    def test_car_validates_price_in_discount_slow3(self,
                                                   validate_price,
                                                   clean_fields):
        fast_price = 80000
        fast = Car(price=fast_price, speed=300)
        slow_price = 5000
        slow = Car(price=slow_price, speed=55)
        Car.objects.add(fast, slow)

        Car.discount_slow()

        self.assertEqual(fast_price, fast.price)  # no discount
        self.assertEqual(slow_price / 2, slow.price)
        Car.save.assert_called_once_with()
        clean_fields.assert_called_once_with(exclude=[])
        validate_price.assert_called_once_with()

Alright, I created a gist with a [sample rst file](https://gist.github.com/douglasrizzo/d1e3e769195e1c9647f7) that `rst-lint` said is clean, but when I uploaded it to pypitest (I just can't afford to keep uploading new versions to pypi), it did not render correctly. At first, `rst-lint` raises a warning, because there is a BibTeX code block, which Pygments cannot pygmentize natively. I installed an additional package and the file was clean, like so:

    $ rst-lint README.rst 
    WARNING README.rst:61 Cannot analyze code. No Pygments lexer found for "bibtex".
    $ sudo pip install bibtex-pygments-lexer
    $ rst-lint README.rst 
    INFO File README.rst is clean.

Notice that this file has no `:linenos:` in it. `rst-lint` raises an error if `:linenos:` is present, but since you asked for an example that passed `rst-lint` and failed to be rendered on PyPI, I removed the statement.
It seems to me, then, that when `rst2html` is able to generate the html documents from the rst files, `rst-lint` is also able to lint these files and say they're clean. The problem is that this html generation process depends on packages installed locally. If the same packages are not present when PyPI renders our readme files, the process will fail remotely, while still being successful (both html generation and linting) on our machines.

Agreed this is a bug with DRF.  While waiting for a DRF fix (I scanned through the DRF issue list and didn't see a matching entry for this bug), what if you did the following:

Instead of validating the list serializer in it's entirety, iterate over each item in the list and validate it individually. As each item is validated, the validated data can be captured in a running validated data list.  Once every item has been validated, the list of validated data is set as the validated data on the original list serializer.  The concept is outlined below.  NOTE: To do this properly, you would not raise an exception immediately if an individual item fails validation as I have done, but rather capture the errors from each item that fails and return a complete error response for the whole list.  

```python
    def bulk_update(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
        partial = kwargs.pop('partial', False)
        # restrict the update to the filtered queryset
        serializer = self.get_serializer(
            self.filter_queryset(self.get_queryset()),
            data=request.data,
            many=True,
            partial=partial,
        )
        validated_data = []
        for item in request.data:
            item_serializer = self.get_serializer(
                data=item,
                partial=partial,
            )
            item_serializer.is_valid(raise_exception=True)
            validated_data.append(item_serializer.validated_data)
        serializer._validated_data = validated_data
        self.perform_bulk_update(serializer)
        return Response(serializer.data, status=status.HTTP_200_OK)
```

Thanks @kthurimella<https://github.com/kthurimella> for the suggestion. Also, see our example Redis implementation, in case it is useful to you: https://github.com/box/box-python-sdk/blob/master/boxsdk/auth/redis_managed_oauth2.py.

@austintrombley<https://github.com/austintrombley> : I wouldn't worry about store_tokens, unless it is something that you know your application definitely needs to do. The average application probably doesn't need to use this feature. And using it unnecessarily or incorrectly can leave your tokens sitting around insecurely, which is bad.

One reason one might use store_tokens is if the application uses multiple processes or is spread out over multiple machines, and needs to coordinate auth. In that case, the process that does token refresh might need to store the new access token in a central location where other processes can access it. For example, see again our example Redis implementation, which I linked above.

Another reason might be to (securely) store the refresh token on the user's disk, that way the application can come back from a restart and continue working, without needing to redo the OAuth2 flow. One way to accomplish this would be with this library: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/keyring<http://cp.mcafee.com/d/1jWVIe418edEIe3AhMVYsCrKrhKUYCYYyyOrhKUYCYYyUUrhKUYCYYyyMrhhvj7cIccI6ZD6GF0_OMamzmyTNVsSJ6J5LzOVJBPnShMV_HYqehPzbP7nKnjpvoV4sUMejpWyaqRQRrCzAtOEuvkzaT0QSyrsdTdTdAVPmEBCb5BFsImCjH3w0eb5BFolYJ6k29K5o-Ium1h6kOrp3BPoVddcS2_id41Fr8l3Y8l3OWNqpeI2Z42FFtd47xUgfYQgltd459Rza14QgjGl-52hEwd5068IFCyAS-Yrnl6Yxydv>. The "Keychain" referred to is the secure storage on Mac OS X, which is used by keyring and other libraries/applications.

These are all examples, not advice. We're not security experts, which is why we don't try to make any suggestions about how to store tokens. Ideally, you wouldn't, unless you really have to, in which case you need to decide what is the most secure approach for your application, target platform, etc.

```python
    class Model(gluon.Block):
        def __init__(self, num_outputs, **kwargs):
            super(Model, self).__init__(**kwargs)
            # use name_scope to give child Blocks appropriate names.
            # It also allows sharing Parameters between Blocks recursively.
            with self.name_scope():
                self.dense0 = gluon.nn.Dense(128)
                self.dense1 = gluon.nn.Dense(64)
                self.dense3_1 = gluon.nn.Dense(num_outputs)
                self.dense3_2 = gluon.nn.Dense(num_outputs)

        def forward(self, x, use_branch1):
            root = nd.relu(self.dense1(nd.relu(self.dense0(x))))
            if use_branch1:
                out = self.dense3_1(root)
            else:
                out = self.dense3_2(root)
            return out
```

My understanding was that **skipped == success**, because when a skipped reaches the end the DAG is marked as success (even with half of my tasks unfinished). And actually this commit 2630361ca24737c28f458825b20ab11c9c996b17 convinced my that I had it right. That's why I wrote this PR.

I understand your point because you actually look at it from the schedulers point of view, I look at it from the users point of view. I already know 2 people in my team that fell into the trap with join nodes. And **documenting it alone isn't enough**, I would then be **prepared to change** this PR into a **raise when we detect** this with ALL_SUCCESS trigger.

Still the first DAG I wrote will never work with the only alternative I have (ONE_SUCCESS), because the branch operator starts a branch that fans out to a lot of parallel tasks that all join together to the same node where the skipped branch arrive. This will never work with the ONE_SUCESS because as soon as one of the parallel branches succeed the rest will just stop. I know I **can hack around this** by introducing a DummyOperator where all successful branches arrive, but I feel dirty if I need to do this.

So I propose this:

- I'll change this PR so the ALL_SUCCESS raises and error.
- Start on another PR that introduces 2 new triggers:
-- ALL_SUCCESS_OR_SKIPPED
-- ALL_FAILED_OR_SKIPPED

What do you think?

No, it's actually part of the Member object and should be assigned by the ZK server.  In our case we just weren't saving the information anywhere it could be accessed later either in the ServiceInstance object or the ServerSet itself.
  You only get the individual ServiceInstances (the twitter interpretation of a zk member node) when you iterate over the ServerSet object at which point you loose the member_id data so rather than changing the type of the returned list I figured it's safest and easiest to just shove the member_id back into the ServiceInstance.
  I chose to make this part of the iterator action because it should remain Group method agnostic should those change or more options be added.
  Because that happens automatically now we also need a way to assign a member_id if we manually join a new instance to our ServerSet object (i.e. we did not get the info from ZK).

Alternatives:
* tld: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/tld
* tldextract: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/tldextract
* publicsuffix: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/publicsuffix/
* publicsuffixlist: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/publicsuffixlist/
* dnspy: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/dnspy/

None of them release a new version every time there is an update to the tld list. If we want that we need to build it. Instead they either require that the user supplies an updated list of that you run an update script before you get started. 

Notes:
* tldextract handles the [differences between ICANN domains and Private domains](https://github.com/john-kurkowski/tldextract/issues/19) properly (exclude the private ones)
* dnspy hasn't been updated since 2013.
* dnspy does not support python 3.
* All of them have permissive licences (MPL, BSD, or MIT).

I think tldextract looks like the best option if we should use an external library.

Resources:
* List of public suffixes: https://publicsuffix.org/list/public_suffix_list.dat
* List of tests here: https://dxr.mozilla.org/mozilla-central/source/netwerk/test/unit/data/test_psl.txt?raw=1

The current proxy and referencing scheme has some limitations with read-only objects, which would be nice to eliminate.  Objects with issues are namely tuples, file objects, database handles, C extensions, and other things of that ilk.  We implement a pickle protocol that can be used to help proxy away these types of objects.

To make things work with the current implementation, the `__getstate__`  and `__setstate__` protocol can be used on a class to proxy these types of objects, and from inside those methods use new instances of the Pickler and Unpickler classes to pickle specific object subgraphs that don't have these issues.

I looked into this error some more.  The function `inline void Write(Value& obj, BufferView& bv, AssetWriter& w)` defined in glTFAssetWriter.inl passes a `size_t` value to `rapidjson::Value::AddMember`, which then tries to construct a `GenericValue` from that `size_t` value.  The `GenericValue` class has constructors for the integral types `int`, `unsigned`, `int64_t` (same as `long long int` on Mac), and `uint64_t` (same as `unsigned long long int` on Mac).  None of those is an exact match for `size_t`, which in this case is the same as `unsigned long int`.

See my comments in #96 and #128. I followed up to see if I could reproduce this issue (or #109). So, I set the interval down to 5s, and since each task took between 30 and 60 seconds (a random delay), the Rabbitmq queue built up gradually. However, once I set the enabled flag to false, the queue drained and the system gradually quiesced itself.

I then set the interval to zero, and as expected, the tasks flowed rapidly:

> [2018-02-15 09:32:39,914: INFO/MainProcess] Scheduler: Sending due task Porsche:w1 (xxx.yyy.zzz)

and the queue built up:

```
Every 5.0s: sudo rabbitmqctl list_queues name messages messages_ready messages_unacknowledged                                         baba: Thu Feb 15 09:37:10 2018

Listing queues
celeryev.26f75434-7b2d-4cb1-9f48-b4d7df05db4e   0       0       0
celeryev.2e2b1d10-e8c9-4892-b672-20f3c1cde813   0       0       0
celery@baba.celery.pidbox       0       0       0

celery  6952    6936    16
```

Again, once I set the enabled flag to false, the queue drained and the system gradually started to quiesce itself. So, with commit 2005bbe, I can reproduce the high task rate only by setting the interval to zero. Then, since my tasks take much longer to run than zero to run, the queue also builds up (i.e. making it look like #128 is an issue).

Finally, using `sudo rabbitmqctl purge_queue celery` facilitated recovery of the system by draining the queue. Thus, I can partially reproduce the symptoms reported here, but only by effectively setting up an unstable setup. With luck, this means that as of at least 2005bbe, I cannot usefully reproduce this.

```
>>> import pathlib
>>> pathlib.Path
<class 'pathlib.Path'>
>>> pathlib.Path('/tmp/test.zip')
PosixPath('/tmp/test.zip')
>>> p = _
>>> f = p.open()
>>> f
<_io.TextIOWrapper name='/tmp/test.zip' encoding='UTF-8'>
>>> f.read(1)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/python2.7/codecs.py", line 314, in decode
    (result, consumed) = self._buffer_decode(data, self.errors, final)
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf8' codec can't decode byte 0xcf in position 12: invalid continuation byte
```

However, opening the file with '`rb`' works for me:

```
>>> p.open('rb')
<_io.BufferedReader name='/tmp/test.zip'>
>>> b = _
>>> b.read(1)
'P'
```

@JordonPhillips Actually, you can do this:

```python
from datetime import datetime
from dateutil.parser import parse
from dateutil.tz import tzutc


d1 = datetime(2002, 10, 2, 13, 0, tzinfo=tzutc())
d2 = parse('Wed, 02 Oct 2002 13:00:00 GMT', tzinfos={'GMT': tzutc()})
print(d1 == d2)
```

But as I detailed on the issue over there, it's not unreasonable to change things later such that this specific example will work, because there's no such time as `'Wed, 02 Oct 2002 13:00:00 GMT'` in the  `GMT0BST` zone (or whatever the TZ string is for London time), and it's probably reasonable to be smart about that if possible.

It looks like this fix is wrong in the sense that it doesn't actually allow round-tripping a `TypeVar` instance through serialization. If we have `T = TypeVar('T')` in a module `foo.py`, what we _want_ to serialize is `foo.T` (so that on deserialization we import `T` from `foo` to get it back), but what we will actually serialize is `typing.TypeVar`, so on deserialization we get the `TypeVar` class rather than the TypeVar instance we had originally.

Unfortunately we can't store `foo.T` to enable the normal import-based deserialization, because a `TypeVar` instance doesn't know the module where it was defined. We could special-case TypeVar further such that we also store its name, then we could at least re-create a TypeVar of the same name on rehydration (would require special-casing TypeVar on both the serialization and deserialization ends). This fix would be easily unit-testable, even without being able to create a full tracing repro: just create a TypeVar instance and round-trip it through `type_to_dict` and `type_from_dict` and assert we've still got a TypeVar of the same name.

But it may not be all that important to get this piece right, because generally the user code we want to trace should not be passing around TypeVar instances at runtime. (I doubt that either of you actually cares to generate stub for the `typing` module, so this trace it's choking on isn't even interesting to you.) So the other problem I'm interested in tracking down here is, why does this call trace get recorded in the first place, when it occurs in the standard library, which should be excluded by the default code filter? It's going to be hard for me to track that down without a repro case, but here's some info that could help:

1. Are you (either of you) using a custom MonkeyType config?
2. Assuming no custom config: when you repro in your own codebase, if you can catch the failure in pdb, can you go up the stack trace and figure out how the trace made it through this check? https://github.com/Instagram/MonkeyType/blob/master/monkeytype/config.py#L105

My best guess is that you might have the third-party `typing` module installed as a zipped egg (or maybe you're on a Python install where the entire stdlib is installed zipped??), and so the code object has no `co_filename` and thus the code filter defaults to accepting it.

@prattmic for now I would encourage you to only use the `virtualenv` script, without any version suffix. This is introduced because wheel files do not contain dynamic code to determine the *installing* python python (they are designed not to have to run package code to be installed). Version suffixes still work correctly with `pip` wheels, but that is because pip itself has a hack to correct them when it recognises it's installing itself. We intend to remove the version suffixes in the future.

Twisted only finds out about child process termination on Windows if [all standard input and output handles remain attached](https://github.com/twisted/twisted/blob/67d1c0fba3efd2b06b4c0c877c595bae30b52724/src/twisted/internet/_dumbwin32proc.py#L400-L406). I also noticed an unexpected dirty reactor failure under Windows on the first test that runs against a child process.

Related to #238.

> I can see potentially adding a partition_offsets argument to commit_offsets so that this logic would not have to be limited only to self._partitions

If I'm understanding correctly, I don't think this would work. The other running consumers wouldn't know that you've done this and would just keep consuming at their current offsets. When they commit they'd overwrite whatever new offset you'd written out for them. Thinking on it this way, I think that forcibly taking ownership of all of the partitions is the only real way to reset their offsets without having your work just be undone again.

One saving grace is that this doesn't have to be atomic (for my purposes anyway) but looking at the [consumer rebalance algorithm](https://kafka.apache.org/documentation.html#impl_consumerrebalance) it doesn't look like "insisting" on a particular ownership result is a thing that's built in.

What seems to be happening is that we use the `ForeignKeyRawIdWidget` but aren't adding the field to `raw_id_fields`. If you look at how Django displays `raw_id_fields` by default you will only see the lookup link (magnifying glass)  but not the add-link (plus) or edit-link (pencil).

This seems to be the relevant code passage in Django: https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/contrib/admin/options.py#L154

So the quick fix would be to always also add our CropFKs to the `raw_id_fields` (and consequently remove the broken extra links)

On the other hand the behavior in `related-widget-wrapper.js` should probably consider the fact that the tag could be something else than a `select`.  So I guess one could argue that it's a bug in Django: Either the additional links shouldn't be displayed for the widget (even if the widget isn't explicitly added to `raw_id_fields`) or the links should be functional.

It is not that the advantages you listed are untrue in Python, it's that the Python culture is different, that's all. Wrapping a group of parameters into a self-validating object is a very Java thing to do, not so much in Python. But I will address your advantages because I did state that I dislike this pattern. 

I dislike it because it adds new Classes, and I'm not sure it's worth it. The one big win for this issue is if we can create a global Parameters object to contain all the model params (for sp, tm, encoders, etc) that can validate itself against all the algorithm configurations. But I'm not sure this can be accomplished without first instantiating the algorithms themselves and running some of their code. This would be inappropriate intimacy of the Parameters class because it must know about the algorithms.

> 1. This can be done whether or not state is pulled out of the algorithms.

I don't see this as an advantage. 

> 1. One central place to see all the algorithm parameters.

Only if we create on global "model params" class, which is only applicable in the OPF. There are so many way to configure a Network, I don't think I'd like to do this at the Network API level. We want to keep the Network API flexible and powerful. 

> 1. Parameters can be applied to more than one setup with and then "applied" in cookie stamp fashion without having to manually configure each algorithm instantiation.

I can do this easily enough today with a little bit of code. 

> 1. More intelligent use of defaults and more flexible application of parameters can be innovated (i.e. getting parameters from distributed locations).

I think the algorithm classes should decide what the defaults are. But then I think the algorithm classes should also do the validation, not a params object. And your 2nd point IMO is over-engineering for a problem we don't have. 

> 1. Makes it easier to eventually develop "sanity protocols" for handling parameter expectations and methods that deal with this.

This would be part of the validation, which IMO should be done in the algorithm classes. 

* * *

So that's my $0.02, but interested to hear other @numenta/nupic-committers speak up about it.

Here is the server:

```python
#!/usr/local/bin/python

import os, os.path, sys, logging
from phue          import Bridge
from gevent        import socket
from gevent.server import StreamServer

# This is adapted from
# https://github.com/gevent/gevent/blob/master/examples/echoserver.py
#
# this handler will be run for each incoming connection in a dedicated greenlet
def handler(socket, address):
    global bridge
    fileno = socket.fileno()
    log.debug('New connection on %d' % fileno)
    # using a makefile because we want to use readline()
    rfileobj = socket.makefile(mode='rb')
    while True:
        line = rfileobj.readline()

        if not line:
            log.debug('client %d disconnected' % fileno)
            break

        msg = line.strip().lower()

        if msg == 'on':
            on_state = True
        elif msg == 'off':
            on_state = False
        elif msg == 'toggle':
            on_state = not bridge.get_group(1, 'on')
        else:
            log.error("received invalid message '%s' on %d" % (msg, fileno))
            socket.sendall("ERROR: invalid message '%s'" % msg)
            continue
        
        log.debug("processing '%s' on %d" % (msg, fileno))

        bridge.set_group(1, { 'hue': 33862, 'sat': 50, 'bri': 254, 'on': on_state }, transitiontime=0)

    rfileobj.close()

logging.basicConfig(format='%(asctime)s %(message)s')
log = logging.getLogger('toggle_hue_daemon')
log.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

bridge = Bridge('rafael_living_hue')

if not os.path.exists(os.path.expanduser('~/run')):
    os.mkdir(os.path.expanduser('~/run'), 0700)

sock_file = os.path.expanduser('~/run/toggle_hue.sock')

if os.path.exists(sock_file):
    os.remove(sock_file)

server_sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_UNIX, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
server_sock.bind(sock_file)
server_sock.listen(25)

log.debug('Starting hue control daemon on %s' % sock_file)
server = StreamServer(server_sock, handler)

try:
    server.serve_forever()
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    log.debug('hue control daemon exiting')
    exit(0)
```

From our experience using a custom tool ([schematic](https://github.com/mozilla/schematic)), i would advise against it.

The main problems we encountered were:
- migrations are written manually, and are thus error prone
- no easy/automatic way to revert a migration (django south provides one for example)
- it depends on the file name (and the number in it) to keep track of the latest migration that ran (which is a problem if you have migrations in different PRs that you don't merge in the correct order, or that run in the wrong order)

There are a lot of corner cases and hard problems to fix while creating such a migration tool, so even though I don't know what solutions are already existing (appart from [nashvegas](https://github.com/paltman/nashvegas) and [south now in django's core](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/topics/migrations/), both specific to django), I would highly recommend not implementing your own system.

Back from vacation :-)

### Identifiers

I think it makes sense to have a uuid/integer based unique identifier for communities. This is also analogous to how GitHub handles repositories - (id, name, full_name - see https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-communities). If you rename a repo the id stays the same, and name/full_name changes, this makes integration with github a lot more reliable in e.g. invenio-github. I.e. I think we have the following two identifiers

- community id: uuid (new)
- community slug: current community id (e.g ``biosyslit``)

### Record metadata and indexing
Would a solution work were we only store the community id in the record, and when indexing a record expanded the community ids to slugs?

Pros:
- Renaming of a community slug would not require record metadata updates.
- Faceting and searching like ``communities:biosyslit`` would still work with ES query parser, and a renaming would just require reindexing affected records (i.e. no extra record metadata revision for large number of records)
Cons:
- Community slug is not part of the record metadata for preservation purposes

### PIDStore vs Community model
I'm somewhat undecided on this. It would introduce some extra complexity which might be overkill for communities, on the other hand we're getting redirection for renamed communities for free via the resolver.

I did some digging on this.

First, apart from the few framework specific hooks in the linked `SSLKillSwitch.js` file, this collection of hooks seem to also be Frida implementations of the SSLKillSwitch2 project (which is what the hooks objection uses are also based on). I am actually unsure what could have caused that to work and not those that is in objection already. (more on that in a bit*).

Inspecting the application in questions' behaviour, I could see a number of clear text HTTP API requests being made where pinning is obviously not a problem. Of the few HTTPS requests that **are** made, none appear to be pinning the remote certificate and seem to be quite happy with my vanilla proxy setup (using Burpsuite). 
Based on section [9.2](https://dji.retroroms.info/howto/iosfrida) of your wiki article, you make mention of the fact that requests to a specific hostname failed. So, I dug a little deeper to try and find out how requests to this URL is handled. An instance of the `DJILogEvent` class is initialised and set up. Once the request is made, `[DJILogEvent sendCommonData]` is called, which in turn calls `[ASIHTTPRequest requestWithURL:r24];` and dispatches it as an asynchronous request.

As far as making requests with `ASIHTTPRequest` go, the only thing I could find was the fact that it validates the legitimacy of the servers certificate and was not configured to pin anything (that I could find quickly anyways).

I double checked my setup to see why I was able to see the requests sent to the hostname in question and realised that it may be related to the fact that I trust my proxies CA certificate. Removed the CA certificate and finally was experiencing the same behaviour as you reported.

(the more bit*) Could you double check that your mobile device trusts your intercepting proxies CA certificate? I have a feeling that this is the only validation that is done for the application in question for HTTPS requests.

Hello! Thank you for giving djoser a try.

Since Django 1.10 there are some changes relating to auth backends:
> The new AllowAllUsersModelBackend and AllowAllUsersRemoteUserBackend ignore the value of User.is_active, while ModelBackend and RemoteUserBackend now reject inactive users.
> https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/releases/1.10/#django-contrib-auth

So, in case of `django.contrib.auth.backends.ModelBackend` which is a default authentication backend your inactive user credentials are rejected during the `django.contrib.auth.authenticate` function call, and so that's why you have received such response. The best solution would probably be to simply change authentication backend to something more fitting - in your case it would probably be `django.contrib.auth.backends.AllowAllUsersModelBackend`.

Our fonction that does version comparison in buildozer (to know if updating of dependencies is needed) doesn't like _rc1, in fact, it doesn't like anything that's not a dot or a number, we need a better function that understand the various ways people express versions, and it's actually quite a complicated problem, we had a look at using LooseVersion from distutils, but it's not able to correctly sort that either (it doesn't crash, but returns the wrong result, that is, `LooseVersion("23.0.0_rc1") > LooseVersion("23.0.0") == True`, clearly an rc is always anterior to its equivalent non-rc release. Since we don't control these versions (it's from the android team at google), we need a robust way to solve this problem.

My ugly workaround currently involves changing `_process_version_string` in `buildozer/targets/android.py` to make it ignore '_rc1'.

```
version = [int(i) for i in version_string.replace('_rc1', '').split(".")]
```
