Any user of the API would still read the documentation for the function in which you of course explain what it can return. If it only ever returns 1 and 0, say so. It doesn't matter that the type is 32bit. That's not a problem to users.

> I'm just asking if your patch allows to link to library individually but you seem to suggest that it does not below.

Ah.  Right, what is currently in Brotli doesn't provide a way to link to each library individually through pkg-config.  Obviously you can still link to the libraries directly, though.

> I don't know what is "most common" for you, but for example in web engines we don't care about the encoder library we just want the decoder to use the WOFF2 fonts.

I'm talking about projects, not user base.  Anecdotally (I'm *way* too lazy to try to do a real survey), I think a lot more projects will want to use both.

That said, I don't have any objection to the idea of adding a few more pkg-config to satisfy the unidirectional use case.  I just want to make sure that's what @eustas wants before I put together a PR so I don't waste effort.

> I'm not saying autotools or autogen must not be used. I'm just saying that we already have some build systems

You understand that I'm not suggesting adding an autotools build system, right?  configure-cmake is just [a shell script](https://github.com/nemequ/configure-cmake/blob/master/configure) you can drop into a CMake project (replacing the existing, somewhat useless, [configure](https://github.com/google/brotli/blob/master/configure) script) which maps an autotools-style interface to CMake; for example `../configure --prefix=/usr --disable-debug CC=clang` just calls something like `cmake .. -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=/usr -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -DCMAKE_C_COMPILER=clang`.  autogen.sh wouldn't be necessary for brotli.  In other words, the entire patch would replacing the `configure` script in brotli with the one in configure-cmake.

>  and docs so that should be enough for people to figure out who to create encore/decoder libraries.

CMake's CLI is a huge pain, and the documentation sucks.  `cmake --help` is almost completely useless, and it's impossible to be sure which variables effect the build without looking through `CMakeLists.txt`; non-brotli-specific docs may or may not apply and are incomplete and/or contain irrelevant information, and the docs in brotli's README are very incomplete (though better off than premake or bazel).

An autotools-style configure script, OTOH, has a useful `--help` and the options are standard and well-known to the point where many (most?) people won't even need documentation.

> Of course we can add more if people really want but that's a separate issue and not the goal of this pull request IIUC.

To be fair, I don't think pkg-config is really the point, either.  AFAICT the real point was creating and installing separate encoder/decoder libraries, which Brotli already does (now, not when this PR was filed).  Beyond that, the only stuff he mentioned was pkg-config and autotools.

Anyways, I don't really care about either issue.  IMHO adding some more pkg-config files and an autotools style interface for the build system are both good ideas, but neither effects me.  Whatever everyone else wants to do is fine.

At the moment I have quite complex shell scripts to configure environment variables or to perform some environment-related set-up.

A good example at the moment is Solr. When you want to customize Solr (100% of the times unless you are just trying it out) you are encouraged to source [this file](https://github.com/apache/lucene-solr/blob/master/solr/bin/solr.in.sh). Many tools provide this as a hook for customization, just a file you source with your environment.

This leaves you with the following:
```
tool-name
  <sources> tool-name.in.sh
```

Quite often your `.in.sh` needs more than just "hardcoding the values". As an example, you might have parts of the configuration in different systems, a database, other services in your DC, etc.
At this point, it becomes quite often to have a script with functions you include in your `.in.sh`

```
# tool-name.in.sh
source too-name-plumbing.sh

XYZ = `function_in_plumbing_to_get_xyz`

# Notify of startup
function_in_plumbing_to_notify_startup ELAPSED_TIME
...
```

OK, I'd to use Python in those situations, the most basic idea is: Create a Python CLI that exposes those variables/functions and at that point, I remember google-fire.

I can know define all those nasty functions I was defining in my plumbing file with python and just expose all that I have defined in my file with google fire! And what is even better, those functions can be used from other python scripts (as fire is triggered only when the script is run as __main__).

There is only though one last nasty part missing. On one scenario it makes sense to pass the arguments as kwargs whilst in the other (sourcing and plumbing) all is usually done via environment variables. The start scripts set many of those and if I find quite "dirty" to have my plumbing scripts accessing os.environ all around.

If only we could have the arguments to default to environment variables! Then all functions nicely express their dependencies and can be used both when scripting/development and when used to enrich a sourced file.

I'd like to use this in many scenarios as such. Example `.bashrc`, I can have a .bashrc.py and code all my logic there. The functions in .bashrc.py beautifully expose their dependencies in the environment and those are magically passed with Fire with this PR.

If this kicks off I'd probably send another PR to have a documented/fixed way to list all commands available in your Fire app. As at that point the .bashrc can become just a: Go to this fire app and set the result of all commands in environment variables.

Bye Bye Shell script

# Closing:

I can "live" without it as at the moment I just have a decorator I put to all functions but that breaks other parts of Fire. Having it built-in would be amazing! Other tools like [click](http://click.pocoo.org/5/arguments/#environment-variables) have the option to default to env vars on a per argument basis. Given the nature of google-fire it would be amazing to have a generic solution.

If it sounds good I'd like to do a lightning talk in PyConUK about how to kill sh with Python and mention google Fire.

Something fishy is going on here...

When I run the example in README.md I get precisely the same results
from great_circle and great_circle_haversine, i.e.,537.1485284062816
miles.  And yet the README.md claims a *different* result for
great_circle_haversine, namely 537.882275111 miles (which happens to be
wrong!).

Also, why the comarison with Vincenty?  One computes the distance on the
sphere and the other on the ellipsoid.

The current formula used by great_circle is just as accurate as the
haversine formula for distances up to a quarter meridian and *more*
accurate than the haversine formula for distances between a quarter and a
half meridian.  For example:

great_circle reports the distance between (-30,0) and (29.999,179.999)
as 20020.578816010657 km.

great_circle_haversine reports the distance as 20020.57881599349 km.

The actual distance (for radius 6372.795 km) is 20020.578816010657667 km
+/- 1 picometer.  So the error in the great_circle result is about 1
nanometer and the errror in the great_circle_haversine result is about
17 micrometers.

In addition, there's no point in junking up the user interface with two
equivalent methods of calculating the same quantity.

So unless there's some fundamental gap in my understanding (which is
entirely possible), I recommend sticking with the existing great_circle
method.

I’m looking into implementing this feature, in order to support another one, involving delayed replicas #36. IMO the non-voting member should continue returning its actual position. As an example, consider  a non-voting replica with a 1 hour delay: its lag from the master should be monitored the same as for the normal replica, only with a different delay limit to alert about. ATM the information about current xlog positions stored in DCS is not used by Patroni, which relies on the data obtained from the API call, but a monitoring system might decide to use the DCS data, as it can read the information about the whole cluster at once (at the expense of obtaining the information with up to loop_wait delay, which is 10 seconds by default).

In order for the other replicas to avoid deferring the master role to the non-voting one, we should expose the non-voting status in the information about the replica returned by the API, and use it during the check for the healthiest node, performed by each replica.

Pure python modules (those which only contain Python code and not compiled extensions) are interpreter-independent.  Compiled extensions, such as _sigmefile, link against libpython are therefore may become interpreter dependent if the Python interpreters were compiled differently. For example if different Unicode support schemes are used, UCS2 vs UCS4, then the module can only be used with interpreter against which the module was linked.

So yes, I expect that a version of Py-ART compiled against Canopy may not work when used with the system Python.  Using only a single Python interpreter is recommended,but uninstalling the other interpreter is not need, removing it from the PATH should suffice.

A good method of checking which interpreter Py-ART, or another compiled modules, is compiled against is to see which libpython the module is linked against using the **ldd**.  If the module is linked against the libpython in /usr/lib or /usr/local/lib then the module is compiled against the system Python.  If the libpython library is in an Anaconda or Canopy directory then it is compiled against Continuum's or Enthoughts' Python.

For example here is the results from a Py-ART _sigmetfile compiled against Anaconda's libpython:

```
~$ ldd /home/jhelmus/anaconda/envs/sandbox/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyart/io/_sigmetfile.so
	linux-vdso.so.1 =>  (0x00007fff569fe000)
	libpython2.7.so.1.0 => /home/jhelmus/anaconda/envs/sandbox/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyart/io/../../../../libpython2.7.so.1.0 (0x00007f4ac358e000)
	libpthread.so.0 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libpthread.so.0 (0x00007f4ac3354000)
	libc.so.6 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6 (0x00007f4ac2f95000)
	libdl.so.2 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libdl.so.2 (0x00007f4ac2d91000)
	libutil.so.1 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libutil.so.1 (0x00007f4ac2b8d000)
	libm.so.6 => /home/jhelmus/anaconda/envs/sandbox/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyart/io/../../../.././libm.so.6 (0x00007f4ac290a000)
	/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2 (0x00007f4ac3bef000)
```

And here is one compiled against the system Python:

```
~$ ldd ~/python/pyart/pyart/io/_sigmetfile.so 
	linux-vdso.so.1 =>  (0x00007fff0c505000)
	libpython2.7.so.1.0 => /usr/lib/libpython2.7.so.1.0 (0x00007f18434c1000)
	libpthread.so.0 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libpthread.so.0 (0x00007f18432a4000)
	libc.so.6 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6 (0x00007f1842ee4000)
	libssl.so.1.0.0 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libssl.so.1.0.0 (0x00007f1842c86000)
	libcrypto.so.1.0.0 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcrypto.so.1.0.0 (0x00007f18428ab000)
	libz.so.1 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libz.so.1 (0x00007f1842693000)

	libutil.so.1 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libutil.so.1 (0x00007f184228c000)
	libm.so.6 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libm.so.6 (0x00007f1841f8f000)
	libgcc_s.so.1 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libgcc_s.so.1 (0x00007f1841d79000)
	/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2 (0x00007f1843c6c000)
```

I my system (Ubuntu 12.04) both of these work as both the system Python and the version of Anaconda I installed support UCS4 Unicode.  If I had an interpreter which had UCS2 support then these modules would not work.  You can check which Unicode support scheme a Python interpreter support using  `python -c "import sys; print(sys.maxunicode)"`.  If that command print 111411 then Python supports UCS4, if 65535 then UCS2.

I think I found where the coercion from bytes to unicode is happening.
 
https://github.com/adobe-type-tools/afdko/blob/71c8d99f088fcf8b1159584d53937ade650534ea/FDK/Tools/SharedData/FDKScripts/MakeOTF.py#L781

in order to get the current working directory, you're using `os.getcwdu` which returns a unicode string, instead of `os.getcwd` that returns a bytes string:
https://docs.python.org/2/library/os.html#os.getcwdu

Then this unicode `currentDir` is used later, for exampe inside `writeOptionsFile`, and is concatenated with other _bytes_ strings, which may contain non-ascii characters. When this happens, the bytes string is implicitly decoded to unicode using the default python encoding (not to be confused with the filesystem encoding) which is "ascii". In general, it's preferable to avoid this implicit coercion and instead only concatenate unicode to unicode or bytes to bytes, and do the decoding/encoding explicitly.

Also, you may have noticed that in python2 you can call the `encode` method on a bytes string, which doesn't make any sense! And if fact it was removed in python3.
You "encode" a unicode string to a bytes string. You "decode" a bytes string to a unicode string.
Calling `encode` method on a bytes string in python 2, is the same as first calling `decode("ascii")` with the default encoding to get a unicode string (and this is going to fail if your bytes string path contains Chinese characters), and _then_ calling `encode` method on the unicode string with the desired encoding. It's basically more like "trascoding", because it's a decode+encode, only that the first decode is done using the default encoding.

The following patch allows me to call makeotf with a font path that has non-ascii characters and write the options file without getting the UnicodeDecodeError from the original poster.

```diff
diff --git a/FDK/Tools/SharedData/FDKScripts/MakeOTF.py b/FDK/Tools/SharedData/FDKScripts/MakeOTF.py
index c807749..9bfc8bb 100755
--- a/FDK/Tools/SharedData/FDKScripts/MakeOTF.py
+++ b/FDK/Tools/SharedData/FDKScripts/MakeOTF.py
@@ -550,7 +550,6 @@ def writeOptionsFile(makeOTFParams, filePath):
                data.sort() # sort by order in which the options were added
                data = map(lambda entry: entry[1], data) # reduce list to just the strings
                data = os.linesep.join(data) + os.linesep
-               data = data.encode('utf-8')
                if makeOTFParams.verbose:
                        print "makeotf [Note] Writing options file", filePath
                try:
@@ -778,7 +777,7 @@ def getOptions(makeOTFParams):
                print __usage__
                raise MakeOTFOptionsError
 
-       makeOTFParams.currentDir = os.getcwdu() # Get the absolute path to the current directory.
+       makeOTFParams.currentDir = os.getcwd() # Get the absolute path to the current directory.
        # makeOTFParams.fontDirPath is curently set to "."
 
        defaultScriptWarning = ""
```

I think that for as long as makeotf only supports python2, it's easier to deal with filesystem paths as native (bytes) string: i.e. what you get from sys.argv.
Introducing some unicode filesystem strings (e.g. with os.getcwdu) makes it more complicated because now you have to deal with the interaction between strings which may be sometimes bytes, other times unicode strings.

HBase has their own bespoke RPC system that's actually fairly fast.  The issue for us is, using Google's plumbing, gRPC is the only option to provide the sort of latencies we need.

RESTful HTTP API calls would be better than running a Thrift server in front of HBase, since that adds an extra hop, but that's a fairly low bar.  I think the performance comparison we want to aim for is Happybase against raw HBase.  I believe that uses HBase's binary RPC format, so I don't see how we'd match it without gRPC.

Hi,

`--rnn-*` and `--attention-*` flags do not apply for the transformer and you are right, some learning rate scheduling should help with this (as written in the original paper). Also, layer normalization is always enabled for the transformer in the latest version (unless you choose a `--transformer-pre/postprocess` string not containing 'n')

You can try the following:
```
python3 -m sockeye.train -s train.en \                                                                                                                                                                                                 
                         -t train.de \                                                                                                                                                                                                 
                         -vs valid.en \                                                                                                                                                                                                
                         -vt valid.de \                                                                                                                                                                                                
                         --num-embed 512 \                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
                         --max-seq-len 50 \                                                                                                                                                                                            
                         --encoder transformer --decoder transformer \                                                                                                                                                                 
                         --num-layers 6 --layer-normalization \          
                         --transformer-model-size 512 --transformer-feed-forward-num-hidden 2048 \
                         --transformer-dropout-prepost 0.1 \                                                                                                                                                         
                         --weight-tying-type src_trg_softmax --weight-tying \
                         --clip-gradient -1 --initial-learning-rate 0.0003 --learning-rate-warmup 50000 --normalize-loss \                                                                                                                                                         
                         -o wmt_model
```

This linearly increases the learning rate for 50k updates/batches and then does the default plateau reduce afterwards.
You can also enable label smoothing suggested in the paper with `--loss smoothed-cross-entropy --smoothed-cross-entropy-alpha=0.2`.

@nawuy I believe that row.cells will return a "merged" cell multiple times, once for each cell that is merged into it. Could that be your problem?

@retsyo Like in HTML, there is no explicit element representing a column. A table is a sequence of rows, each of which is a sequence of cells.

To delete a column *n*, delete the *n*-th cell in each row.

Note there is also a `a:tblGrid/a:gridCol` element for each "grid" column and you'll need to delete the right one of those too.

Also note that there is such a thing as a `a:tc` element, but it means "table cell" rather than table column.

A little more on these details is here:  
http://python-pptx.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dev/analysis/shp-table.html

If there are merged cells in the table that may complicate matters.

It follows from the previous comment that you can improve the relative performance of (J)PMML by performing "term selection" in your pipeline.

For example, try replacing `SVC` (uses all terms) with `RandomForestClassifier` (uses a subset of most significant terms), and see how the performance numbers change.

Well, it also follows that the converter should enable normalization when the last step of your pipeline is a "greedy" classifier. For example, in case of `SVC`, the frequencies of all terms in the vocabulary will be computed anyways.

Got it.  I'm not sure what your plan is to handle network issues for ZonePlayers, but I would suggest keeping an eye on the NOTIFY ssdp multicasts.  When a ZonePlayer has an issue, it will drop subscriptions without any type of notification (makes sense).  When it comes back online it will send a ssdp:byebye message, then a ssdp:alive message.  You can observe the sequence numbers in the alive message to determine if a re-subscription is necessary.  Good luck!

Dear @Snevzor, a Bluetooth discovery of the device _is_ necessary before you connect to the device. This is due to the fact that BlueZ (running as a service) stores discovered devices "in your system".

BlueZ's D-BUS API provides methods to add devices without previous discovery but this library doesn't implement such API endpoints yet. Hence you need to have your machine discover your Bluetooth peripheral first.

[UPDATE 5, 23-11-17][11:38PM] [NRF52]
Some good news! With some fixes in the Zephyr code, the BT Module can be made to connect to more than one devices simultaneously. Following are the mods that got it working for me: 
- Add in `..zephyr/samples/bluetooth/hci_uart/nrf5.conf`:
```
CONFIG_BT=y
CONFIG_BT_HCI_RAW=y
CONFIG_BT_MAX_CONN=16
CONFIG_BT_MAX_PAIRED=16
```
- Note that the above specs should be exactly in the same order as above. Might not work if you shuffle them.
- To be double sure, add the above configs to `..zephyr/samples/bluetooth/hci_uart/generic.conf`

Now, generate the HEX and flash the board.
Power off the NanoPi, connect the board and boot. If you see a change in the mac address of the bt board, it has been flashed with the changes. 

Now using `bluetoothctl` CLI, connect to one Nuimo, and wait for a few seconds. Now while the Nuimo is connected, perform:
`[Nuimo]# connect <another_nuimo_mac_addr>` .
This should give you a positive connection message. This means that both Nuimos have been successfully paired. 
**Note:** The DBUS communication is not always perfect when using this board, so it might connect to two Nuimos and then not connect to anymore. 
**Note:** Reconnection of disconnected Nuimos is still not fixed. That has to be fixed from the Senic Hub application stack.

_Function to test_
```python
import saspy
from pandas.io.json import json_normalize

# initialize our session
sas = saspy.SASsession()


def remove_duplicates(data, name, sort_variable):
    """
    Sort and deduplicate normalized data
    using PROC SORT.
    """

    dataframe = json_normalize(data)

    sas.df2sd(dataframe, table=name)

    sas.submit(
        f"""
        proc sort data={name} NODUPKEY;
            by DESCENDING '{sort_variable}'n;
        run;
        quit;
        """
    )   
    
    return sas.sasdata(f"{name}")
```

Your suggestion is valid but the devil is in the details. As you could have noticed, there are basically two large stages in the script: parsing and downloading. The parsing stage produces a structure with nested lists and dictionaries that describes the content to download on the later stage. In parsing, we don't know yet the files names where we will store content to. They are generated during the download stage.

The issue that you have encountered is the result of a quick hack. When I was thinking on how to store assets offline, I decided to simply inline images into HTML. A better way could be to link images in HTML and save images to disk. But again, we don't know the target filenames when parsing. A possible solution might be to return lazy handlers from parsing stage which when are called in download stage, request the necessary resources of a page.

I'm explaining all this to show that I think it's not just a matter of fixing two lines of code. A better design is needed here and it requires some thought.

Here is my view on this:

* there are valid, though probably mostly uncommon use cases for multiple loops
* Twisted made a historic design mistake in not allowing for multiple/different event loops, asyncio is doing it right
* allowing Twisted to work with multiple event loops (reactors) would probably be a pretty big effort, so I wouldn't hold my breath

In principle there seem to be 3 options of what we could do:

a) remove `loop` argument in Autobahn factories, use the `txaio` configured (global) loop (which is fixed for Twisted, and which could be changed for asyncio - but globally) - I think this is what @meejah suggests
b) add `loop` as an argument to _all_ Autobahn factories, also for the Twisted ones, but for Twisted bail out if that is not "the one true reactor" - also hinted at by @meejah 
c) let Autobahn interfaces for Twisted vs asyncio classes diverge (like today)

---

a) covers more use cases: it would allow switching the one global event loop, but it does not allow multiple loops concurrently - neither in one and the same, nor in different threads (unless the loop in txaio would be stored in thread local storage - but that seems to be a can of worms).

b) covers all use cases (for asyncio). However, it would trigger a lot of change in Autobahn both in impl. and interfaces - with practically zero gain for Autobahn itself, and for most use cases.

c) is obviously not so cool. we already have a few places diverged (ApplicationRunner etc), and this doesn't feel right - it's a goal conflict: we want to paper over differences in Twisted/asyncio, without rewrapping everything _and_ expose all framework specific extra features. Mission impossible.

My current understanding of the process model is as follows:

i) main process
ii) SyncManager spawned by main process
iii) Handler process spawned by main
iv) Collector process(es) also spawned by main

Each collector runs in its own process space, so if no collectors are configured, there are no collector processes. As a result, there is always a minimum of 3 processes running (see below).

There's only one handler process used for executing all configured handlers. If no handlers are configured, this process still exists. If 99999... handlers are configured, they all run within this same process.

There is some code in src/diamond/server.py that's executed by the main process and it checks configured collectors vs running collectors and re-spawns any that aren't running - I'd guess this is what you're seeing when randomly killing subprocesses, although I'm not 100% on that. Collectors are always spawned last, so this can be tested out by aiming the SIGTERM at the first two processes created by main and seeing if those re-spawn. If they do, I dunno what's happening and need to take another look at the code. I'm relatively new to Diamond so still learning about its internals :)

I agree that we should take this route @tacaswell. I am however not completely content with the implementation in this PR, as it involves a custom `imageio`-like baseclass, which might be sensitive to future changes in `imageio`.

I thought of another route: if we would reimplement all our readers as imageio plugins, we'd lose all the fancy/lazy slicing capabilities. To recover that functionality, we want to wrap the imageio readers, something like:

`pims.open = lambda x: WrapImageIOReader(imageio.get_reader(x))`

However, we will lose knowledge of frame shape, pixel dtypes, and dimension names as they are not supported by default in imageio. These can however be guessed by reading the first frame, which is actually what happens already in many of our readers.

Preferably, we could give `WrapImageIOReader` all these properties, and if they do not exist in the underlying pims (or imageio) plugin, they will be investigated automatically by reading the first frame. In this way, we can give our pims plugins the `frame_shape` etc. attributes, while they are not strictly necessary enabling the use of non-pims imageio plugins.

Either way, I am in favour of only making one wrapper that is dimensionality-aware, based on `FramesSequenceND`.

If we decide on something, we should discuss with someone at imageio.

@Natsuke I'm afraid the issue is with the underlying platform, and I'm afraid I don't know the root cause. Glancing at your installation script, things look okay. Issues like this (etcd problems during provisioning) usually point to heavy IO caused by Docker doing a bunch of `docker pull`s on the host to get the Deis components, which prevents etcd from writing its snapshot data to disk in a timely fashion.

We've resolved this on cloud providers by moving the Docker graph (`/var/lib/docker`) to a separate volume than the root volume, which is where etcd is writing its data (`/var/lib/etcd`). I'd suggest you try to do that in your environment.

The latin1 prober is based on the "heuristic table" with letters types/categories (small ascii, capital ascii, small ascii with accute, ...) and it is not normal/common language model. The values >0.73 causes that the latin1 prober wins before other common lang models which can leads to wrong detection. There are many languages in the Latin1 group and the better solution is to create "heuristic table" instead of many language models.

* the `receive_handshake` function will be used as the `client_connected_cb` parameter for the [`asyncio.start_server`](https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/asyncio-stream.html#asyncio.start_server) function.
* Use the `reader` to read the auth init message.
* Use `HandshakeResponder.decode_authentication(auth_init_message)` on the auth init message.
* Some extra changes are likely required to allow retrieval of the remote's public key from the auth init message.
* extract the remote's address via the [`writer.get_extra_info()`](https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/asyncio-stream.html#asyncio.StreamWriter.get_extra_info) API.
* Use `HandshakeResponder.create_auth_ack_message()` to create a reply for the handshake.
* Use the `writer` to send the reply to the remote.
* Use `HandshakeResponder.derive_shared_secrets()` and use the return values to instantiate a `Peer` instance.

@matthieubellon, I was writing a custom field that uses a multiple choice checkbox widget, and saves data in the DB as CSV. I stumbled upon this issue when my choices had integer values. 

Even if I could always coerce the choice values to strs when saving, I didn't have an easy way to know that I needed to cast them back to something other than str when constructing the field/widget. This in turn gave me trouble when trying to display the current value in the widget or when cleaning the widget's value.

In the end, I still used the custom field with the multiple choice checkbox widget, but I enforced that all choice values were strs. Then I added a simple method in the model to get the display value for a given choice (this method read the schema and finds the choice by the given value, and return's it's label).

If you're really stuck on using that ancient container, an upgrade path for you that will work is

```
# Switch to non sudo, create a Python 2 virtual environment 
USER $NB_USER
# Commenting out the conda update things broke!
RUN CONDA_CHANNELS=defaults conda update conda
RUN conda update conda
RUN conda create --name snakes python=2
```

Why is it necessary to have a new `Package` object?  There is already such in object in `conda.resolve`.

This might be a half-baked idea.  It is tied to #1022 and an attempt to get rid of "root" environments.  Instead any arbitrary environment can be the "default" at any time.  Conda would always use its protected "conda-only" environment, but everything else would be whatever the current named "default" environment was.

I think this could be achieved right now simply by following a procedure of:

* miniconda for conda install (root env)
* never touch the root environment except for conda updates (and related conda-specific packages)
* even if only one other environment (say, called "anaconda"), do all package installs there
* in `.bashrc` right after `PATH` do `source activate anaconda`
* now all conda ops are happening on the `anaconda` environment.

I'm happy enough with that proposal that I'll close this.

I still maintain that the example usage here is incorrect. You should not need to set content on token streams passed to analyzers as the analyzer itself will call `set_content(doc.content())` on its own. The bug here was that `ICUTokenizer` did not properly clear any remaining internally buffered content when `set_content()` is called. This resulted in the analyzer seeing extra words that it shouldn't have, but only when constructed with a "dirty" tokenizer (one that has content already set on it).

The `bulk` API has a very different format and doesn't accept raw documents, each document needs to have a metadata line and a line containing data following it - see https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/docs-bulk.html

To make this simpler the python client has a helper for bulk operations - http://elasticsearch-py.readthedocs.io/en/master/helpers.html#bulk-helpers

Hope this helps

For elasticsearch 2.x please use 2.x release of the client as well, this combination is not supported. `delete_by_query` api has been removed in elasticsearch 2.0. If you want to use this functionality you have to either install the plugin and call it manually using `client.transport.perform_request` or implement it yourself using a combination of `scan` and `bulk` helpers from elasticsearch.

recommended way is to organize your data so that you can always just delete entire indices, that is a much faster operation.

Rebasing essentially re-writes a part of the Git history for a particular branch.
Therefore, rebasing someone elses branch 'on behalf' of them could have unintended consequences if their not aware as it's essentially a force-push into their fork.

We *could* merge for them which GitHub already supports but that's different from rebasing - https://blog.github.com/2015-09-03-protected-branches-and-required-status-checks/.

I have a proposal for that.

Right now Aiohttp implements HTTP pipelining but this feature is not fully enabled - correct me if Im wrong @fafhrd91. The default concurrence per connection [1] does not allow Aiohttp to process more than one request at a time. 

Having this scenario and taking into account the complexity that this feature adds in terms of code and the low usage in production systems, almost in microservices architectures,  I would propose to reduce the current HTTP pipelining to a more naive implementation that **does not support concurrence in the same connection**.

Why ? 

1) Reduce the codebase and as a result the performance footprint.
2) Give still support for HTTP pipelining for the none happy path and meeting the HTTP 1.1 requirements.

How ?

1) The first PR, just getting rid of the `max_concurrent_handlers` parameter and removing all of the code that preserves the arrival order and allows Aiohttp have more than one concurrent handler executing at a time.

2) The second PR, reduce if its possible the number of layers needed by request to handle the output.

[1] https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/web_protocol.py#L96

We are reading data via HTTP and get a response which has a `Content-Type` header in the form `application/vnd.foo+json; encoding=utf-8`.

To read JSON data from the response, the full content type has to be specified:
```
    data = response.json(content_type='application/vnd.foo+json; encoding=utf-8')
```
As `json()` already handles the encoding option from the servers `Content-Type` header internally, it feels wrong to specify (and even fix) the encoding through the content type string. If the server leaves out that information some day it will even break the client.

cd notebook
git checkout i3365

Method 2 works because autograd [replaces `np.dot`](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/a7db3e6151deb50a6a64a55b653e43ce58ee4cad/autograd/grads.py#L123) with a version that, when applied, records its operation on the tape. Method 1 does not work because the ndarray methods aren't replaced, so when `w.dot(x)` is evaluated in the forward pass, `w` is a plain `ndarray` and `x` is a node_types.ArrayNode and so the value of the expression is a plain old float. (I think `w` probably sees `x` as a fellow ndarray because [Node classes override getattr](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/a7db3e6151deb50a6a64a55b653e43ce58ee4cad/autograd/core.py#L94-95).)

So my understanding is that, at least for now, for autograd to work, calls to `ndarray` methods need to be replaced with their numpy function call versions, i.e. `np.func` instead of `x.func(y)`. (It's only really necessary for the calls that ultimately get applied to Node types in the forward pass.) To mitigate this problem, autograd could edit the ndarray class like it edits the numpy module (replacing its methods in-place). Another strategy would be to remove these in-place edits entirely and instead extend ndarray and wrap the numpy functions inside autograd, ultimately requiring the user to replace their `import numpy as np` with something like `import autograd.wrapped_numpy as np`.

`np.tensordot` doesn't work because it's not in the [set of supported numpy operations](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/a7db3e6151deb50a6a64a55b653e43ce58ee4cad/autograd/grads.py).

No, that code wouldn't do it: we'd still be running the VJP over the forward pass!

Let's look at the VJP as a function of both `x` and `v`. I agree we want to differentiate wrt `x` (and not `v`). It seems like there must be some way to do it, but I admit my thinking about it was flawed.

Was the only reason to write it that way originally to make it fast?

Just depending on the first does make sense according to numpy's semantics (according to the docstring of `np.argmax`), though I think arguments could be made for following other conventions. The root of the problem is that if we think of `np.max` as a function from R^2 to R, with input (x,y) and output z, then it's not smooth along y=x and so the 'right' gradient is ambiguous. One reasonable thing to do is to output an element of the subgradient, which is the convex hull of the vectors (0,1) and (1,0), and so this new commit would follow that prescription (while the current solution, which effectively returns (1,1), doesn't). Also, since all of the tests pass with this commit, we don't have any documented reason to prefer the current convention to the new convention

To summarize, because @yukoba's commit arguably follows numpy's semantics better, returns an element of the subgradient, presumably fixes his real use case, and doesn't break anything I know of, I vote merge!

Is there any use case we're missing?

Out[3]: 1.0

Out[4]: 2.0

I was a bit confused by this at first - because I thought planemo defines a repository as a directory containing a ``.shed.yml`` file - and for the most part it does. But the shed operations it looks like will just use the supplied path as is if it doesn't find any ``.shed.yml`` files.

```
def _find_raw_repositories(ctx, path, **kwds):
    name = kwds.get("name", None)
    recursive = kwds.get("recursive", False)

    shed_file_dirs = find_matching_directories(
        path, SHED_CONFIG_NAME, recursive=recursive
    )

    config_name = None
    if len(shed_file_dirs) == 1:
        shed_file_dir = shed_file_dirs[0]
        try:
            config = shed_repo_config(ctx, shed_file_dir, name=name)
        except Exception as e:
            error_message = PARSING_PROBLEM % (shed_file_dir, e)
            exception = RuntimeError(error_message)
            _handle_realization_error(exception, **kwds)
            return [exception]
        config_name = config.get("name", None)

    if len(shed_file_dirs) > 1 and name is not None:
        raise Exception(NAME_INVALID_MESSAGE)
    if config_name is not None and name is not None:
        if config_name != name:
            raise Exception(CONFLICTING_NAMES_MESSAGE)
    raw_dirs = shed_file_dirs or [path]
```

This behavior is because of the last line here. I really was trying to take a very light touch with .shed.yml files originally and everything in there truly was optional - non-optional fields could be specified at the command-line for instance.

With that in mind, two things.
- I don't think a missing .shed.yml would be caught by tools-iuc's Travis setup with this change since the jq directory wouldn't have been picked up as a directory/repository to lint. So I'm not sure this helps https://github.com/galaxyproject/tools-iuc/pull/1402. Maybe I'm wrong though?
- Following the linting levels for tools - in my view of things this isn't an error, it is a warning - the repository can still be used - it isn't broken per se. Error the way I've tried to use it (and maybe I've failed to be consistent at times) means usable, completely broken - not just in poor form.
- If we change this from error to warn, the check becomes a much weaker check than what is produced with ``--ensure_metadata`` and that makes sense I think - the whole point of ``.shed.yml`` is provide some default metadata properties.

Therefore - I think the existing INFO or WARN would be correct - the problem is that you are linting in such a way that you are explicitly ignoring warnings. I think it would be better to just fail on warnings as is the planemo default. I've opened a tools-iuc PR here https://github.com/galaxyproject/tools-iuc/pull/1407 that would cause metadata problems with repositories to fail the build while still "ignoring" warnings in the tools themselves - I think that is better right?

We don't have an XSD for ``tool_dependencies.xml`` do we? Anyway, making ``--xsd`` the default behaviour for linting as per #567 is a good plan and would resolve this issue.

I think reactive.Endpoint specializations could be created for a higher level way of creating protocols with common behavior, such as your examples. By not stuffing it all in the base Endpoint class, more advanced behavior and magic could be added without causing problems with interfaces that don't need it. Perhaps a relation that provides and consumes an HTTP endpoint could be as simple as creating a requires.py and provides.py with the following:
```python
class PrometheusMetrics(charms.reactive.endpoint.HTTPEndpoint):
    '''
    Usage:
    >>> ep = reactive.endpoint_from_name('rest')
    >>> ep.set_port(8080)  # Override the default port
    >>> ep.set_path('/metrics')
    >>> ep.url
    'http://10.0.60.44:8080/metrics'
    '''
```

Similarly, a cleaner interface to the Juju networking details could be put in an Endpoint specialization instead of the base class. But I personally think that it is work exposing this information through the base Endpoint because the network details such as ingress address, egress subnets etc. are automatically published for all relations by Juju, and we need to be prompting charm developers to use the new Juju features rather than lazily falling back to hookenv.private_ip_address() and making their charms fail with cross model relations.

Why not make a wrapper that uses correct run dir then? Eg:

```
cmd /c "cd ${workspaceRoot} && python -m pytest --cov=Scripts --cov-config ${workspaceRoot}/.coveragerc --cov-report html:${workspaceRoot}/locale/coverage_html --cov-report xml:${workspaceRoot}/local/coverage.xml --cov-report annotate:${workspaceRoot}/local/coverage_annotate ${workspaceRoot}/UnitTests/"
```

Also, you can remove all the absolute paths, this would work just as well:
```
cmd /c "cd ${workspaceRoot} && python -m pytest --cov=Scripts --cov-report html:locale/coverage_html --cov-report xml:local/coverage.xml --cov-report annotate:local/coverage_annotate UnitTests/"
```
This request seems superfluous, there has to be a more restrictive usecase in order to justify yet another option in pytest-cov.

To replicate the error, add some out-of-tree setup.cfg file
```
[pytest]
python_files = *
```

... to some path `$OTHER_SETUP`, create a virtualenv, and pip install the example project.

The invocations to replicate are then: `./bin/py.test -c $OTHER_SETUP --pyargs mypkg -v` vs. `./bin/py.test -c  $OTHER_SETUP -n8 --pyargs mypkg -v`.

The first command should fail because it's using the `$OTHER_SETUP` file which selects for all python files, although one of the tests really shouldn't be ran. The second command erroneously succeeds because it appears to ignore the bad configuration file.

Hey @nicoddemus, thanks for taking a crack at this. I was just getting started myself on a proposal to solve https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/175.

I wonder if there is a way to cover the common use cases while avoiding adding new options and APIs. Basically, after carefully reading through the history on https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/175, my position is now the same as what @hpk42 described in [this comment](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/175#issuecomment-112200644):

>  I suggest to think how we can enhance behaviour by default, in a "no new option/API" sense as this brings the benefits to everyone.

We should refactor xdist to formalize the concept of a "chunk" of tests--i.e. tests that are always sent to the same node. Initially, every test will be its own chunk, which just yields the behavior we have today. But once we have that internal concept, we can then pass around additional information about what fixtures each test use. This information can automatically be used to chunk tests appropriately, as @hpk42 described in [his comment](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/175#issuecomment-112200644).

If this counter-proposal sounds attractive -- not necessarily as the one we want to go with, but one that should be developed further -- then I am ready to write up a more detailed proposal as well as a candidate implementation. I have the time to iterate and see through a complete solution this month.

I'm worried about the magic that occurs in the `branch` selection, I rather make that an explicit option.

When options are changed the automatic usage of a `-0001` suffix should not occur.
Instead I think the operation should be aborted and resolved with one of the following method.

1. As suggested here by @dusek and @aptalca, a flag to indicate the change in domains, my suggestion would be to call it `--modify`

2. Use option `--alias` or `--branch` (assuming these option-names are not in use today) to select the name of the folder to store the keys. This would replace the today automatic suffix generation.

Both option 1 and 2 above would work independently.
But at least one should know where the certs will be stored before running the client.

I think you misunderstand the "today" situation. The default argument to the "default" argument is the current date (frankly, a bit of an unfortunate choice in my opinion). So if you pass it "Today at 5PM", it will give you the current date at 5 PM, true, but if you pass it "Tomorrow at 5PM" or even just "5PM", it will also give you the current date at 5PM.

If you use `fuzzy_with_tokens`, you'll see which parts of the string the parser ignored. "Today" will be in that list.

Greetings. I really think that latter method is too complicated for such task. Pros - it's more explicit. Cons - more work to support and implement (cycles that mentioned by @embray, for example), if processor you're depending on is renamed or not exist you should throw an error? And what if processor is applied dynamically on schema instance creation? You need priorities (or order) only for schema sub-classing and mixins. It's really not such a big job to determine integer for it (and you need it in your own code, in most usecases). But on using "after/before" it would not be obvious when two processors without "after/before" is executed (at least I can't get straight picture right now). So in the end - we would get more explicit(+) but more complicated(-) behavior. About third-party libraries with several processors - in such rare cases - they may use "order" with step 10 to allow user-code insert their processors when needed (for example popper.js use step 50 to allow user-code insert "modifiers"). So my vote - to merge this pull request as is (maybe rename "priority" to "order"). Thank you guys for all your work!

Another thing to note is that the 32-bit build configurations I have in the proto-type don't work :(

The main reason is that the even though I've used a multilib gcc (version of GCC that supports building 32-bit code) I don't have 32-bit versions of python/mono/java (etc...) installed so the 64-bit python/mono/java can't load the 32-bit `libz3.so` library.

One solution is to simply not test the python/.NET/java APIs in this configuration. Another (which is more complicated) is to tell docker to use a base image that is 32-bit which will therefore give us 32-bit builds of everything. The problem with this approach is there aren't any official 32-bit ubuntu docker images so we'd have to maintain our own image for this purpose. There are plenty of unofficial images though (e.g. https://hub.docker.com/r/delcypher/ubuntu32/ )

> But the sigmoid(x1)-sigmoid(x2) indeed works and is meaningful?

No, this could become negative. You need to come up with something different. And depending on how you do it, the distance measure may become more difficult to interpret. For sigmoids, cross-entropy is probably easier to justify than Euclidean distance. Also think about the learning dynamics: For sigmoids with Euclidean distance, for a pair of points, it's easier to move the one that is closer to the center of the hypercube (the gradient is larger), creating some possibly unwanted asymmetry.

> The transform is highly non-linear anyway, why we care about the linearity of the last NN layer?

True. If the previous to last layer is sigmoidal, adding a linear layer doesn't really make the Euclidean distance more justifiable. If you have rectifiers in the hidden layers, it may be different.

Fish's answer is pretty comprehensive, but here's my ten-second version - angr has several types of values, and one of them is a "Symbolic Bitvector", or BVS. You can just plop one into memory or registers wherever you like and the symbolic execution engine will treat it like any value.

If this value is a null-terminated string and you'd like it to be a specific length, you can add constraints that the body bytes should be nonzero and the last byte should be zero.

> First of all, I want to say that I run angr on Windows7, because I need to write a thesis about PE malware analysis.

You can run angr under Linux or macOS and analyze Windows binaries there. The types of binaries angr can analyze is agnostic of the platform on which angr runs.

> Therefore, I would like to confirm whether it is impossible to use SimFile on Windows currently.

The problem is that our emulated Windows environment (`SimWindows` in [simos/windows.py](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/simos/windows.py)) does not take an `fs` in its `state_blank()` method. I think it can be implemented in a similar way as in `SimLinux` (see [simos/linux.py line 170](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/simos/linux.py#L170)).

We should probably implement `fs` support for `SimUserland.state_blank()` and `SimUserland.state_entry()` - or even for `SimOS`. This can be a great entry-level angr development task for whoever wants to pick up.

Deleting an url could be made failsafe by copying URL into meta info.

- Playlist is created using just required part of JSON/YAML format playlist
- Playlist is imported and mpsyt generates meta data, and each metadata entry has it's URL.
- Both URL array and metadata are hashed, and hashes are stored as "last known good configuration"
- An entry is removed from URL array or from meta data section. Based on hash of section, we can determine which section changed and ask user what to do, to remove the entry from the other section, to undo change or to add new data (new meta entry, or new url).

This scenario is a bit outstretched and I wouldn't support it in early implementations. I would split this into 2 or 3 feature request/todo items.

I would once again emphasise that I think that it's the best to support raw URL list format just for import/export and generate JSON/YAML style playlists which are still easy to handle manually.

OK, I've made so some additional progress.

I've identified a flush command which cascades into a lot of possible exceptions which are correctly caught (this wasn't 'just' a blind ```try: ... except: pass``` ) and processed within the SubJobXMLList class which deals with writing subjob data to disk.
This particular problem generated a __large__ number of exceptions which were expected in some contexts but not the case that was causing slowdowns. This scales strongly with the number of subjobs in a Job.
Slightly oddly this only occurs in the session where the job has initially been created.

After some minor re-writing of how this class is used I've managed to significantly reduce the length of time associated with flushing large complex jobs which have just been created.
This is bug which is present in the SubJobXML class which was introduced in 6.1.xx and is needed for the faster loading of Ganga at startup.


In combination with this I was able to submit a large number of jobs and after some testing I was able to get:

10 jobs to the localhost backend in 14sec
100 jobs to the localhost backend in 4min


Submitting the same LHCb job as described above:
__1:20min__ BK Query
__25sec__ upload a Dirac file
__15sec__ requesting and processing replicas
__45sec__ splitting of subsets
__30sec__ submitting 11 subjobs to Dirac using 3 in parallel

Total __3:15min__ down from 5:10min.
(It's not clear whether the excessive flush commands were disabled in the above profile I ran but I'll assume that it was as I'm not sure)

Additionally Running:
```_args=[[i] for i in range(0, 100)]; j=Job(); j.splitter=ArgSplitter(); j.splitter.args=_args; j.submit()```
runs in __30sec__ to submit 100 jobs to the localhost backend.

Using this I was able to submit 1000 jobs to the localhost backend in 26min. This isn't the fastest of tasks, but 24min of this was spent splitting the subjobs. Submission of 1000 subjobs to the localhost takes <2min.

My local repo is a bit dirty at this point so I'm going to need some time to clean up the fixes before they're fully uploaded but I'll mention here when this has been done.

@fedorov - I am not familiar with Slicer, but everything looks fine to my eyes. The image below shows the raw DICOM loaded into Osirix and the converted NIfTI loaded into MRIcro. Note that the alignment matches and the bright spot is on the left. With Slicer it does not appear to rotate the NIfTI image to the nearest orthogonal, but if you load the render view you can see from the balls that the coordinates appear fine. The default coordinate for DICOM is to have rows go from top-to-bottom, the way you read english, whereas the default with NIfTI is to have the rows ascend with larger values further up the Y axis from relative to smaller values, the way you would draw a graph with cartesian coordinates. The good news is that the precise rotation is stored in the SForm of the NIfTI header, so while it is appropriate to reverse the row order between formats the final image stores this information. 
  https://www.slicer.org/wiki/Coordinate_systems
Perhaps you could add a feature request for Slicer to have the ability to load images in a default format. For example, the code below losslessly reorients a volume to match the nearest orthogonal to an identity matrix and then saves the residual rotation in the matrix. This ensures that images appear roughly aligned without interpolation and retaining rotation information.
  https://github.com/neurolabusc/MRIcro/blob/1bb6fa088a0ec61bbdd93f89b1c0c54677b0bc76/MRIcroX/nii_ortho.cpp

Okay, thanks for the additional information - I could also repro this in a python 3 environment and it turns out to be a python 2-3 compat issue stemming from some TensorBoard code that works only in python 2.  I'll revise the issue title accordingly and this should be a pretty simple fix.

Essentially, the issue is the following line of code:
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/0.1.8/tensorboard/plugins/text/text_plugin.py#L170

The use of `np.dtype(str)` is unsafe because it means "null-terminated bytes" in python 2 and "little-endian UTF-32" in python 3.  This becomes an actual problem because of the use of `asstring()` which is just a further source of confusion - it's been renamed `asbytes()` in recent numpy because it actually returns "the raw data bytes in the array".  The result is the following behavior:

```
python2> np.array('foo', np.dtype(str)).tostring()
'foo'
python3> np.array('foo', np.dtype(str)).tostring()
b'f\x00\x00\x00o\x00\x00\x00o\x00\x00\x00'
```

In python 2, tostring() == the raw bytes which works because numpy's storage format matches python's.  In python 3, the raw bytes don't match python - every character is followed by 3 null bytes due to the UTF-32 encoding.  This in turn causes the markdown library to see non-word characters surrounding every letter in the string, which means that intra-word underscores like `f_g_h_2017` are no longer detected as "word-surrounded underscores" by the `smart_emphasis` feature and thus do get treated as italics.  The null bytes evidently get dropped before HTML rendering, so nothing else broke.

The `mSlaac` field in `otBorderRouterConfig` gives control over whether or not Thread devices should locally autoconfigure an address from the associated IPv6 prefix.  Currently, OpenThread will always autoconfigure an address if the `mSlaac` flag is set.

Question: Should OpenThread make the application/client responsible for configuring IPv6 addresses for prefixes other than the Mesh Local prefix?  The advantage of this approach is that the application/client is responsible for determining what IPv6 prefixes it should configure addresses for.

I'm glad to hear that this work have a chance to be merged, encouraging ;) Indeed, the plan is for it to be completely compiled out by default, with no cost.

As for the old & new mixing, I don't have a solid plan yet - I'm also afraid that it won't be possible, at least in some edge cases. I wasn't aware of the Security Policy TLV details, it's R bit seem indeed very interesting solution.

Since many internal details of Thread working are still unknown to me, I'm not sure how much we will gain by that. Like, I'm not sure how much Route64 TLV processing is done on children / REED. It seems that almost none, but the spec also says that child need to check cost to leader from its parent Route64 TLV. If that is the case, even the Security Policy TLV won't help, we will have to transmit values in some backward compatible format (which will be extremely annoying). 

However, Security Policy TLV is probably the most elegant solution (if it will work) - and it feels like designed for case like that, actually. It would allow introducing new version of Route64 TLV without any tricks to maintain compatibility with 1.1 - therefore saving some bytes.

Ok, I found a hack-y solution.  

Instead of using setuptools "entry_points" (the preferred approach for making executables) I can use setuptools "scripts", which then allows me to write a custom setup script to launch one of the GUIs using pythonw.  The downside with this approach is that I use this line to set the environment:

/usr/bin/env pythonw

which grabs whatever Python is in the $PATH.  This is good in development but bad in production.  Pip generally installs python scripts with the path to the Python you used to download that package hard-coded at the top, so:  /Users/nebula/anaconda2/bin/python  instead of /usr/bin/env python.  What this means is that our installed script might be more prone to breaking if the user tries a different Python that isn't compatible.  explained better by someone else here:

#!/usr/bin/env python executes the system's default Python version. That is fine as long as your code is compatible with said version. When the default version is updated (from 2.5 to 3, say) your code or other Python code which references /usr/bin/env may stop working, even though the old Python version is still installed. For that reason it makes sense to "hardcode" the path to the appropriate python interpreter.    http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1530702/dont-touch-my-shebang

Anyway, what this means to me is that we should add Anaconda compatible scripts (that may be slightly less stable) to the existing pip package.  From a UI perspective, what should we call those scripts?  In testing I called the executables things like pmag_gui_a (a for anaconda), but maybe something else would be better?  (Note that we can't use a command line argument here to specify anaconda vs non-anaconda -- it has to be the actual name of the executable).  I would love input on this naming issue.  I have pushed to master changes in command_line_setup.py and adding a bin/ directory with the Anaconda scripts if anyone wants to have a look.  

You can also pip install a mostly-Anaconda compatible pmagpy-cli from the test PyPI site:  

 pip install -i https://testpypi.python.org/pypi pmagpy-cli --upgrade 

That install should work with Anaconda with the commands pmag_gui_a, demag_gui_a, etc.

Also, I've found several more incompatibilities with the Anaconda implementations of the GUIs.  I'm going to keep working on those problems, and create new issues for any bugs that I need help with.  Slow progress!

I don't know how much Linux-specificity bullet we're willing to bite here (the relevant functionality might not even be available in psutil itself on some other systems?) but I found that you can look in `/proc/net/fd` for a line that contains

`:3039 00000000:0000`

where :3039 is the hexadecimal value of the local port you're interested in, and then read the `inode` field from that line, and then look at the link target of every single file in `/proc/*/fd` to see if it is a link to that numbered inode. This is apparently how `lsof` does it (from `strace lsof`) and maybe also how psutil is doing it.

If you restrict mode is enable, that is the default behavior, there is a limit:
* Obfuscated script can't imported from non-obfuscated scripts.
That is, you CAN NOT disassemble obfuscated module "mymod" by "dis", because no any other script can import "mymod" out of obfuscated scripts.

Refer to [Restricted mode](https://github.com/dashingsoft/pyarmor/blob/master/src/user-guide.md#restrict-mode)

But if you disable restrict mode, you can import obfuscated scripts from other script.  The byte code of code object will be restored after it's first called, so you can disassemble it.

Kernel -> interrupt sends Ctrl-C to the process and would not usually kill the kernel.

`concurrent.futures` does not provide a way to terminate the executor (e.g. [this discussion](https://mail.python.org/pipermail/python-dev/2014-March/133697.html). According to the documentation, `ProcessPoolExecutor.terminate` 

> Signal the executor that it should free any resources that it is using when the currently pending futures are done executing. 

Now, suppose we have 20 tasks, 10 completed, 4 running, 6 pending, the 4 running ones would be interrupted (and kills R), but in theory the 6 pending ones will be running. Then the master process calls `shutdown`, which should in theory wait for the 6 pending to complete, but somehow failed to do so (which is caused perhaps by the fact that the worker is in a broken status due to interruption).

So in the end I think `multiprocessing.Pool` gives us more control over the subprocesses and we should stick to it despite of the remaining problems.

netCDF is used to serialize xarray to or deserialize it from a disk file.

According to the reference, the pickled object's format may not be backward compatible, so for compatibility, the to_dict and from_dict are preferred.

For performance, I did a quick experimentation:

```
In [9]: %timeit -n 10 ray.put(data)
219 ms ± 13.9 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10 loops each)

In [10]: %timeit -n 10 ray.put(data)
301 ms ± 66.9 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10 loops each)
```

The above is the to_dict and from_dict version, while the latter is the pickle version.

So I've transformed the PR to use the to_dict and from_dict methods.

I don't think this belongs into Streamlink itself. The [Streamlink Twitch GUI](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink-twitch-gui) does have a Twitch chat function for opening browser windows or other chat application. So if you want, you can use it instead of the Streamlink CLI.

These minimal browser window layouts you are seeing on your screenshot are being created by already running browser instances and websites which are using a Javascript API for that ([DOM Level 1 window.open](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Window/open)). You can't simply launch any browser process like that.

Chromium and Chrome do have a parameter that lets it launch in a new so called app session (`--app="URL"` parameter), but other browsers like Firefox require addons, Edge and Safari don't support minimal window layouts at all and MSIE requires a VBScript launch script. I'm not sure if Opera has support for this and then there are also countless of other browsers and browser forks.

Also figuring out which browser is being used by the user is next to impossible. There are simply too many different setups and configurations.

If you want to open a Twitch chat window while using the Streamlink CLI, you can write a simple shell wrapper script that receives a Twitch channel name as parameter and launches Chromium/Chrome and Streamlink.

**twitch.sh**
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash

chromium "--app=https://twitch.tv/${1}/chat" 2>&1 >/dev/null &
streamlink ${@:2} "twitch.tv/${1}" best
```
**Example:**
`twitch.sh CHANNEL -p mpv`

@windsoilder the idea is that

```python
with MultiError.catch(RuntimeError, handler):
    ... body ...
```

is a substitute for

```python
try:
    ... body ...
except RuntimeError as exc:
    handler(exc)
```

The work the same except the first one handles `MultiError`s.

When you talk about filtering `Cancelled`, you're thinking of what cancel scope blocks do. `MultiError` itself doesn't know anything about cancellation. In fact, `open_cancel_scope` uses `MultiError.catch` to catch `Cancelled` exceptions. So that's a higher level than all this.

yeah, this is one awful workaround for python limitations colliding with another awful workaround for python limitations.

the short answer is that you can work around it by having your trio runner wrap the synthetic function inside a do-nothing pass-through function:
```python
async def call(async_fn, *args, **kwargs):
    return await async_fn(*args, **kwargs)

trio.run(call, actual_async_fn)
```
(I guess you could sort out your problem with kwargs here too actually, kill 2 birds with 1 stone.)

The long answer is: handling control-C correctly is hard, because if it arrives while we're running user code (e.g. that infinite loop that you accidentally wrote, oops) then we want `KeyboardInterrupt` to be raised immediately, but if we're in the guts of trio then we *definitely don't* want `KeyboardInterrupt` to be raised because that could corrupt our internal state and break everything. So trio installs a signal handler for SIGINT, and when the user hits control-C the handler has to figure out whether we're in user code or trio internals. The only possible way to do this is by walking the stack looking for a marker saying "entering user code" or "entering trio code", and it turns out that the only thing in a stack trace that we have control over and can use as a marker is: the frame's locals. (This is ugly and still subject to a small unfixable race condition in some cases; IMO python frames should hold a reference to the function object so we can check it for attributes, see #79. But for now this is what we're stuck with.)

So currently our marker is a non-string object, to make sure that we can't possibly accidentally collide with any *real* local variable, and this gets injected into the top frame of every (non-system) task. (And this is why adding a trivial wrapper function will let you dodge the issue – we'll inject it into the wrapper function's stack frame, rather than the user code's frame.)

You definitely don't want to copy this into the IPython user namespace... I can't actually think of anything that would break, but given the weird stack walking stuff we have to do, letting this variable leak into other parts of the stack is not going to promote stability and predictability.

Unfortunately there's a confusing detail here that I should maybe have
pointed out more clearly: the cancel scope and nursery fixtures actually
*need* to receive any exceptions raised while they're active. For cancel
scopes, this is so they can look at passing Cancelled exceptions and decide
to either catch them or not. For nurseries, it's because every nursery has
an implicit cancel scope, so the same logic applies. Given this, I think
we're stuck: it means that if a fixture contains a nursery, we *must* throw
in any exceptions we get, but if a fixture is written in the usual pytest
style, then we *must not* throw in any exceptions because that will prevent
teardown. (This is the point I was making with the 'tempfile' example
above.) Do you see any way around this?

For the redundant free scheme to work, we just need the representations of the block_ids to be sortable (although it's also good for speed considerations if they are hashable).

So, we could just have the `block_id` be a tuple of `(block_counter, rule_id)`

Having it be an array provides some difficulties as we will often want to serialize block_ids to databases which have uneven support for arrays.

Alternatively, we could have `block_id = block_counter * 100 + rule_id`. If a set is not empty, then we can extract this with `rule_id = block_id % 100`

The downside of this is that we are placing an effective limit on the number of blocking rules we can have. Whether that's a real problem I don't know.

If the C-extension is shared as (I understand) the doc tells it, calling `initr()` only once should work.

You can check it easily, as `rpy2` is maintaining R's initialization status (since it is unfortunately not part of R's C API):

```python
rpy2.rinterface._rinterface.is_initialized()
```

However, calling `initr()` multiple times should not matter, unless initialization options are changed between calls and the code relies on the changes: R will only be initialized the first time `initr()` is called.

Although frameworks are heading in this direction, I think it's a mistake to assume all backends support implicit broadcasting. Caffe2 certainly doesn't, and PyTorch only got numpy-style broadcasting recently. And the details of what broadcasting is supported can be subtle (do you broadcast on only one dimension, or all mismatching dimensions) and often frameworks have some legacy behavior that doesn't match the original broadcasting rules shoehorned in because they couldn't break BC.

So I'm definitely sympathetic to the idea that it should be obvious, without shape inference, whether or not broadcasting took place.

The problem is that you are using `run` instead of `run_smartseq2`.
`run` has a more tricky set of inputs you need to get right.

The usage is
```
Usage: velocyto run_smartseq2 [OPTIONS] BAMFILES... GTFFILE

  Runs the velocity analysis on SmartSeq2 data (independent bam file per cell)

  [BAMFILES, ...] a sequence of bam files to be analyzed (e.g. use a wild-card expansion)

  GTFFILE genome annotation file

Options:
  -o, --outputfolder PATH  Output folder, if it does not exist it will be created.
  -e, --sampleid PATH      The sample name that will be used as a the filename of the output.
  -m, --repmask PATH       .gtf file containing intervals to mask
  -d, --dump TEXT          For debugging purposes only: it will dump a molecular mapping report to hdf5. --dump N, saves a cell every N cells. If p is prepended a more complete (but huge) pickle report is printed (default: 0)
  -v, --verbose            Set the vebosity level: -v (only warinings) -vv (warinings and info) -vvv (warinings, info and debug)
  --help                   Show this message and exit.
```
Furthermore you can pass al the bamfiles at the same time using wildcard (glob) expansion.
